Question: The distance moved by the particle in time t is given by x = t<sup>3</sup> – 12t<sup>2</sup> + 6t + 8. At the instant when its acceleration is zero, the velocity is

A ladder 10 metres long rests with one end against a vertical wall, the other end on the floor. The lower end moves away from the wall at the rate of 2 metres/minute. The rate at which the upper end falls when its base is 6 metres away from the wall, in M/min is :
Solution:
\[\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = 2\]
\[{x^2} + {y^2} = 100\]
\[y = \sqrt {100 - {x^2}} \]
\[\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = \frac{{ - x}}{{\sqrt {100 - {x^2}} }}\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}\]
\[ = - \frac{6}{8} \times 2\]
\[ = - \frac{3}{2} \Rightarrow \]y falls at the rate of \[\frac{3}{2}m/\min \].

Question: The function \[y = \frac{{2{x^2} - 1}}{{{x^4}}}\] is
Options: a decreasing function for all x \[ \in \] R – {0}
a increasing function for all x \[ \in \] R – {0}
Solution:
\[y = \frac{2}{{{x^2}}} - \frac{1}{{{x^4}}}\]
\[\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{ - 4}}{{{x^3}}} + \frac{4}{{{x^5}}}\]
\[ = \frac{4}{{{x^3}}}\left( {\frac{1}{{{x^2}}} - 1} \right)\]
\[ = \frac{{4\left( {1 - {x^2}} \right)}}{{{x^5}}}\]
\[ = \frac{{ - 4\left( {x + 1} \right)\left( {x - 1} \right)}}{{{x^5}}}\]
\[\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} > 0\]

for \[x \in \left( { - \infty , - 1} \right) \cup \left( {0,1} \right)\]

Question: The surface area of a cube is increasing at the rate of 2 cm<sup>2</sup>/s. When its edge is 90 cm, the volume is increasing at the rate of (in cm<sup>3</sup>/sec)
Solution:
\[s = 6{l^2}\]
\[\frac{{ds}}{{dt}} = 12l\frac{{dl}}{{dt}}\]
\[2 = 12 \times 90 \times \frac{{dl}}{{dt}} \Rightarrow \frac{{dl}}{{dt}} = \frac{1}{{6 \times 90}}\]
$v = {l^3}$
So, \[\frac{{dv}}{{dt}} = 3{l^2}\frac{{dl}}{{dt}} = 3 \times 90 \times 90 \times \frac{1}{{6 \times 90}} = 45c{m^3}/\sec \]

Question: If function \[y = f\left( x \right)\] has a second order derivative \[f''\left( x \right) = 6\left( {x - 1} \right)\]. If its graph passes through the point \[\left( {2,1} \right)\] and at the point the tangent to the graph is \[y = 3x - 5\], then the function is \[{\left( {x + a} \right)^b}\]. The value of \[a + b\] is
Solution:
\[f''\left( x \right) = 6\left( {x - 1} \right)\]
\[f'\left( x \right) = 6\left( {\frac{{{x^2}}}{2} - x} \right) + c \Rightarrow f'\left( 2 \right) = c\]
Now, it is given that \[y = 3x - 5\] at \[x = 2 \Rightarrow y'\left( 2 \right) = 3\]
\[ \Rightarrow 3 = c\]
So,
\[f'\left( x \right) = 3{x^2} - 6x + 3\]
\[ \Rightarrow f\left( x \right) = {x^3} - 3{x^2} + 3x + d\]
So, it is given that \[y = 3x - 5\] at \[x = 2 \Rightarrow y\left( 2 \right) = 1\]
\[ \Rightarrow 1 = 8 - 12 + 6 + d\]
\[d = - 1\]
So,
\[f\left( x \right) = {x^3} - 3{x^2} + 3x - 1\]
\[ = {x^3} - 3x\left( {x - 1} \right) - 1\]
\[ = {\left( {x - 1} \right)^3}\]

Question: The value of constant $c$ such that the straight line having slope $-1$ and $y$-intercept $3$ is tangent to the curve \[y = \frac{c}{{x + 1}}\] is
Solution:
Given curve is, $y = \frac{c}{{x + 1}}$
Equation of tangent is, $y = - x + 3$
On solving these, we get
$ - x + 3 = \frac{c}{{x + 1}}$
$ \Rightarrow - {x^2} + 2x + 3 = c$
$ \Rightarrow {x^2} - 2x + c - 3 = 0$
Condition of tangency, $D = 0$
$ \Rightarrow 4 - 4\left( {c - 3} \right) = 0$
$ \Rightarrow 1 = c - 3$
$ \Rightarrow c = 4$

Question: Let f (x) = (1+b<sup>2</sup>) x<sup>2</sup> + 2bx + 1 and m (b) the minimum value of f (x) for a given b. As b varies, the range of m (b) is
Solution:
\[m\left( b \right) = \frac{{\left( {4{b^2} - 4 - 4{b^2}} \right)}}{{4\left( {1 + {b^2}} \right)}}\]
(since min value of a quadratic is given by \[\frac{{ - D}}{{4a}}\])
\[m\left( b \right) = \frac{1}{{1 + {b^2}}}\]
\[\infty > {b^2} \geqslant 0\]
\[\infty > 1 + {b^2} \geqslant 1\]
\[0 < \frac{1}{{1 + {b^2}}} \leqslant 1\]

Tags.Subject: Math
Tags.Topic: global extreme values in an interval

Question: The equation of the tangent to the curve \[y = \sqrt {9 - 2{x^2}} \] at the point where the ordinate and the abscissa are equal, is
Solution:
\[\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{ - 2x}}{{\sqrt {9 - 2{x^2}} }}\]
When
\[x = \sqrt {9 - 2{x^2}} \] (Given)
\[9 - 2{x^2} = {x^2}\]
\[3{x^2} = 9\]
\[x = \sqrt 3 \] (As y can not be negative)
So,point is \[\left( {\sqrt 3 ,\sqrt 3 } \right)\]
Hence, \[\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{ - 2\sqrt 3 }}{{\sqrt 3 }} = - 2\]
So ,Tangent is
\[y - \sqrt 3 = - 2\left( {x - \sqrt 3 } \right)\]
\[2x + y - 3\sqrt 3 = 0\]

Question: The intercepts on x-axis made by tangents to the curve, \[y\, = \,\int_0^x {|t|\,dt,} \,\,x\, \in \,R,\] which are parallel to the line y = 2x, are equal to
Solution:
\[\because {\text{y}} = \int\limits_0^ \times {\left| {\text{t}} \right|} \,{\text{dt}}\]
\[ \Rightarrow \frac{{{\text{dy}}}}{{{\text{dx}}}}{\text{ = }}\left| {\text{x}} \right|\]
But \[y = 2x \Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = 2\]
\[ \Rightarrow \left| x \right| = 2 \Rightarrow x = \pm 2\]
Points \[{\text{y}} = \int\limits_0^{ \pm 2} {\left| {\text{t}} \right|} \,{\text{dt}}\,{\text{ = }} \pm \,{\text{2}}\]
\[\therefore \]equation of tangent is
\[{\text{y - 2 = 2 (x - 2)}}\;or\;{\text{y + 2 = 2 (x + 2)}}\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,x{\text{ - intercept}} = \pm 1\]

Question: The maximum value of the function $f\left( x \right) = 2{x^3} - 15{x^2} + 36x - 48$ on the set $A = \left\{ {x|{x^2} + 20 \leqslant 9x} \right\}$ is
Solution:
The given function is $f\left( x \right) = 2{x^3} - 15{x^2} + 36x - 48$ and $A = \left\{ {x|{x^2} + 20 \leqslant 9x} \right\}$
$\begin{gathered}
   \Rightarrow A = \left\{ {x|{x^2} - 9x + 20 \leqslant 0} \right\} \\
   \Rightarrow A = \left\{ {x|\left( {x - 4} \right)\left( {x - 5} \right) \leqslant 0} \right\} \\
   \Rightarrow A = \left[ {4,5} \right] \\
\end{gathered} $
Also, $f'\left( x \right) = 6{x^2} - 30x + 36 = 6\left( {{x^2} - 5x + 6} \right) = 6\left( {x - 2} \right)\left( {x - 3} \right)$
Clearly $\forall \,\,x \in A,\,\,f'\left( x \right) > 0$
$\therefore \,\,f$ is strictly increasing function on A
$\therefore \,\,$ Maximum value of $f$ on A
$\begin{gathered}
   = f\left( 5 \right) = 2 \times {5^3} - 15 \times {5^2} + 36 \times 5 - 48 \\
   = 250 - 375 + 180 - 48 = 7 \\
\end{gathered} $

Tags.Subject: Math
Tags.Topic: global extreme values in an interval

Question: A triangular park is enclosed on two sides by a fence and on the third side by a straight river bank. The two sides having fence are of same length x. The maximum area enclosed by the park is
Solution:
\[{\text{AT = x}}\,{\text{sin}}\,\alpha \]
\[{\text{BT = x}}\,{\text{cos}}\,\alpha \]
Area of triangle ABC
\[ = \frac{1}{2}{\text{base}} \times {\text{height}}\]
\[{\text{ = }}\frac{{\text{1}}}{{\text{2}}}{\text{(2BT)(AT)}}\]
\[{\text{ = }}\frac{{\text{1}}}{{\text{2}}}{\text{(2}}{{\text{x}}^{\text{2}}}\,{\text{cos}}\,\alpha \,\,{\text{sin}}\,\alpha {\text{)}}\]
\[ = \frac{1}{2}{x^2}\,\sin \,2\alpha \,\, \leqslant \,\frac{1}{2}{x^2}\,{\text{as}}\,\, - \,1\, \leqslant \,\sin \,2\alpha \, \leqslant 1\]
\[\therefore \]maximum area of \[\Delta {\text{ABC}}\,{\text{ = }}\frac{{\text{1}}}{{\text{2}}}{{\text{x}}^{\text{2}}}\]

Options: increasing for certain range of x

Question: If A > 0, B > 0 and A + B \[ = \,\frac{\pi }{3},\] then the maximum value of tan A tan B is
Solution:
Let y = tan A tan B
\[\because \;B = \frac{\pi }{3} - A\]
\[\therefore \;\tan B = \frac{{\sqrt 3 - \tan A}}{{1 + \sqrt 3 \;\tan A}}\]
\[ \Rightarrow y = \operatorname{tanA} \left( {\frac{{\sqrt 3 - \operatorname{tanA} }}{{1 + \sqrt 3 \tan {\text{A}}}}} \right)\]
\[ = \left( {\frac{{\sqrt 3 \operatorname{tanA} - {{\tan }^2}A}}{{1 + \sqrt 3 \tan {\text{A}}}}} \right)\]
\[\frac{{dy}}{{d{\text{A}}}} = \frac{{{{\sec }^2}{\text{A}}\left( {1 + \sqrt 3 \operatorname{tanA} } \right)\left( {\sqrt 3 - 2\operatorname{tanA} } \right) - \sqrt 3 {{\sec }^2}A\left( {\sqrt 3 \tan {\text{A - }}{{\tan }^2}{\text{A}}} \right)}}{{{{\left( {1 + \sqrt 3 \tan {\text{A}}} \right)}^2}}}\]
\[ = \frac{{{{\sec }^2}{\text{A}}\left( { - \sqrt 3 {{\tan }^2}A - 2tanA + \sqrt 3 } \right)}}{{{{\left( {1 + \sqrt 3 \tan {\text{A}}} \right)}^2}}} = 0\]
Put tan A = x
\[ - \sqrt 3 {x^2} - 2x + \sqrt 3 = 0\]
\[\left( { - \sqrt 3 x + 1} \right)\left( {x + \sqrt 3 } \right) = 0\]
\[x = \tan {\text{A = }}\frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}\]
\[ \Rightarrow \tan B = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}\]
\[ \Rightarrow y = \frac{1}{3}\]

Question: The function \[f\,\left( x \right) = \frac{{\sin \,x}}{x}\] is decreasing in the interval
Solution:
\[f'\left( x \right) = \frac{{x\cos x - \sin x}}{{{x^2}}}\]
\[f'\left( x \right) < 0\]
\[x\cos x < \operatorname{sinx} \]
For, \[x \in \left( {0,\frac{\pi }{2}} \right)\]
\[\begin{gathered}
  \tan x > x \\
   \Rightarrow x \in \left( {0,\frac{\pi }{2}} \right) \\
\end{gathered} \]
For \[x \in \left( { - \frac{\pi }{2},0} \right)\]
\[\tan x > x\]
No solution
For \[x \in \left( {\frac{\pi }{2},\pi } \right)\]
\[\tan x < x\]

Question: If the ratio of base radius and height of a cone is 1 : 2 and percentage error in radius is \[\lambda \% \], then the error in its volume is
Solution:
\[\frac{r}{h} = \frac{1}{2}\] ..(i)
\[\frac{{\Delta r}}{r} \times 100 = \lambda \]
\[v = \frac{1}{3}\pi {r^2}h\]
\[ = \frac{1}{3}\pi {r^2}.2r\] (using (i))
\[ = \frac{2}{3}\pi {r^3}\]
\[\frac{{dv}}{{dr}} = 2\pi {r^2}\]
\[\frac{{\Delta v}}{{\Delta r}} = 2\pi {r^2}\]
so, \[\frac{{\Delta v}}{v} \times 100 = \frac{{3\left( {2\pi {r^2}} \right).\Delta r}}{{2\pi {r^3}}} \times 100\]
\[\begin{gathered}
   = 3.\frac{{\Delta r}}{r} \times 100 \\
   = 3\lambda \% \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question: The circumference of a circle is measured as 28 cm with an error of 0.01 cm. The percentage error in the area is
Solution:
\[2\pi r = 28\,cm\]
\[\Delta \left( {2\pi r} \right) = 0.01\]cm
\[2\pi \Delta \left( r \right) = 0.01\]
\[A = \pi {r^2}\]
\[\frac{{dA}}{{dt}} = 2\pi r\]
so, \[\Delta A = 2\pi r\Delta r\]
Hence,
\[\frac{{\Delta A}}{A} \times 100 = \frac{{2\pi r\Delta r}}{{\pi {r^2}}} \times 100\]
\[ = 2\frac{{\Delta r}}{r} \times 100\]
\[ = \frac{1}{{14}}\]

Question: If f (x) = x<sup>3</sup> + 4x<sup>2</sup> + \[\lambda \]x + 1 is a strictly decreasing function of x in the largest possible interval [–2, –2/3] then
Solution:
\[f'\left( x \right) \leqslant 0\]
\[3{x^2} + 8x + \lambda \leqslant 0\]
\[\begin{gathered}
  3\left( {{x^2} + \frac{{8x}}{3} + \frac{\lambda }{3}} \right) \leqslant 0 \\
  3\left( {{x^2} + \frac{{8x}}{3} + \frac{\lambda }{3} + \frac{{16}}{9} - \frac{{16}}{9}} \right) \leqslant 0 \\
  3{\left( {x + \frac{4}{3}} \right)^2} + \left( {3\lambda \frac{{ - 16}}{9}} \right) \leqslant 0 \\
\end{gathered} \]

So,
\[\begin{gathered}
   - 2\frac{{ - 2}}{3} = \frac{{ - 8}}{3} \\
   \Rightarrow \left( { - 2} \right)\left( {\frac{{ - 2}}{3}} \right) = \frac{\lambda }{3} \\
  \lambda = 4 \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question: The curve y – e<sup>xy</sup> + x = 0 has a vertical tangent at
Solution:
\[\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} - {e^{xy}}\left[ {y + x\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}} \right] + 1 = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} - \left( {x + y} \right)\left[ {y + x\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}} \right] + 1 = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \left( {1 - {x^2} + xy} \right)\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = xy + {y^2} - 1\]
So, \[\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{1}{0}\] (since vectical tangent slope \[ = \frac{1}{0}\])
\[ \Rightarrow \frac{{xy + {y^2} - 1}}{{1 - {x^2} + xy}} = \frac{1}{0} \Rightarrow {x^2} + xy = 1\]

Question: The maximum value of xy subject to x + y = 8, is
Solution:
\[z = x\left( {8 - x} \right) = 8x - {x^2}\]
\[\begin{gathered}
  \frac{{dz}}{{dx}} = 8 - 2x = 0 \\
  x = 4 \\
\end{gathered} \]

\[{z_{\max }} = 4 \times 4 = 16\]

Question: The minimum value of \[{2^{{{\left( {{x^2}\, - \,3} \right)}^3}\, + \,27}},\] is
Solution:
\[{x^2} \geqslant 0\]
\[{x^2} - 3 \geqslant - 3\]
\[{\left( {{x^2} - 3} \right)^3} \geqslant - 27\]
\[{\left( {{x^2} - 3} \right)^3} + 27 \geqslant 0\]
\[{2^{{{\left( {{x^2} - 3} \right)}^3} + 27}} \geqslant 1\]

Question: The maximum area of the rectangle that can be inscribed in a circle of radius r, is
Solution:
Area = xy = \[4{r^2}\cos \theta \sin \theta = 2{r^2}\sin 2\theta {\text{ }}\]
(since length of rectangle = $ = x = 2r\cos \theta $ and
width $ = y = 2r\sin \theta $)
\[\frac{{dA}}{{d\theta }} = 4{r^2}\cos 2\theta {\text{ }} = {\text{ }}0\]
\[ \Rightarrow 2\theta = \frac{\pi }{2}\]
\[ \Rightarrow \theta = \frac{\pi }{4}\]
\[\frac{{{d^2}A}}{{d{\theta ^2}}} = 8{r^2}\sin 2\theta < 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \theta = \frac{\pi }{4}\] is a point of maxima.

Hence , Max Area \[ = 2{r^2}\]

Question: The maximum value of x<sup>3</sup> – 3x in the interval [0, 2], is
Solution:
\[f\left( x \right) = y = {x^3} - 3x\]
\[\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = 3{x^2} - 3\]
\[ = 3\left( {x + 1} \right)\left( {x - 1} \right)\]

\[f\left( { - 1} \right) = 2\]
\[f\left( 2 \right) = 2\]
So f(x) has max. at x = -1 & f (-1) = 2

Question: The length of subtangent to the curve x<sup>2</sup> y<sup>2</sup> = a<sup>4</sup> at the point (–a, a) is
Solution:
\[2x{y^2} + 2{x^2}y\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = 0\]
\[m = \frac{{ - y}}{x} = \frac{{ - a}}{{ - a}} = 1\]
So, Sub tangent = a
(since length of subtangent = \[\frac{y}{{dx/dx}} = \frac{a}{1} = a\])

Question: If the normal to the curve y = f (x) at the point (3, 4) makes an angle \[\frac{{3\pi }}{4}\] with the positive x-axis, then f ’ (3) is equal to
Solution:
As Slope of normal = –1 \[\left( {\tan \frac{{3\pi }}{4} = - 1} \right)\]
\[f'\left( 3 \right) = 1\] (slope of tangent = 1)

Question: The angle of intersection of the curve y = x<sup>2</sup> & 6y = 7 – x<sup>3</sup> at (1, 1) is
Solution:
\[\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = 2x\] and \[6\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = - 3{x^2}\]
\[{m_1} = 2\] \[ \Rightarrow {m_2} = \frac{{ - 1}}{2}\]
\[{{\text{m}}_{\text{1}}}{{\text{m}}_{\text{2}}} = - 1\]
\[ \Rightarrow \theta = 90^\circ \]

Question: If a particle moving along a line follows the law \[s = \sqrt {1 + t} ,\] then the acceleration is proportional to
The function f (x) = 2x<sup>3</sup> – 3x<sup>2</sup> – 12x + 4 has
Solution:
\[f'\left( x \right) = 6{x^2} - 6x - 12\]
\[ = 6\left( {{x^2} - x - 2} \right) = 0\]
\[\left( {x + 1} \right)\left( {x - 2} \right) = 0\]

So f(x) has maxima at x = -1 & minima at x = 2.

Question: A line is drawn through the point (1, 2) to meet the coordinate axes at P and Q such that it forms a \[\Delta \]OPQ, where O is the origin, if the area of the \[\Delta \]OPQ is least, then the slope of the line PQ is
Solution:
Equation of line is \[{\text{y - 2 = m (x - 1)}}\]
\[P = \left( {\frac{{ - 2}}{m} + 1,0} \right),Q = (0,2 - m)\]
Area of \[\Delta {\text{POQ = }}\frac{{\text{1}}}{{\text{2}}}{\text{(OP)(OQ)}}\]
\[{\text{ = }}\frac{1}{2}\left( {1 - \frac{2}{m}} \right)(2 - m)\]
\[ = 2 - \frac{m}{2} - \frac{2}{m}\]
Let \[f(m) = 2 - \frac{m}{2} - \frac{2}{m}\]
\[f'(m) = - \frac{1}{2} + \frac{2}{{{m^2}}} \Rightarrow {m^2} = {\text{ }}4 \Rightarrow m = \pm 2\]
$f''\left( m \right) = - \frac{4}{{{m^3}}}$
$f''\left( 2 \right) < 0$ and $f''\left( { - 2} \right) > 0$
\[ \Rightarrow \]Area will be least at \[{\text{m = - 2}}\]
\[ \Rightarrow \]Slope of line \[{\text{ = - 2}}\]

Question: The minimum value of x<sup>x</sup> is attained (where x is positive real number) when x is equal to :
Solution:
\[y = {x^x}\]
\[\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = {x^x}(1 + \ln x) = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \;\ln x = - 1\]
\[ \Rightarrow \;x = {e^{ - 1}}\]
\[ \Rightarrow \;x = \frac{1}{e}\]

Question: Water is dropped at the rate of 2 m<sup>3</sup>/sec into a cone of semi vertical angle of 45°. The rate at which periphery of water surface changes when height of water in the cone is 2 meters is
Solution:
\[\frac{{dv}}{{dt}} = 2\]
\[V = \frac{1}{3}\pi {h^3}\left( {{\text{As}}\,r = h} \right)\]
\[\frac{{dv}}{{dt}} = \pi {h^2}\frac{{dh}}{{dt}} = 2 \Rightarrow \frac{{dh}}{{dt}} = \frac{2}{{\pi {h^2}}}\]
\[h = 2 \Rightarrow \frac{{dh}}{{dt}} = \frac{1}{{2\pi }}\]
Now,
Periphery \[P = 2\pi r = 2\pi h\]
\[at\,\,h = 2 \Rightarrow \frac{{dp}}{{dt}} = 2\pi \frac{{dh}}{{dt}} = 1\]

Question: The radius of a sphere is changing at the rate of 0.1 cm/s. The rate of change of its surface area when the radius is 200 cm, in cm<sup>2</sup>/sec is:
Solution:
\[\frac{{d{\text{x}}}}{{dt}}{\text{ = 0}}{\text{.1cm/sec}}\]
\[s = 4\pi {r^2}\]
\[\frac{{ds}}{{dt}} = 8\pi r\frac{{dr}}{{dt}}\]
\[{\text{ = 8}}\pi \times 200 \times 0.1\]
\[ = 160\pi \,\,c{m^2}/\sec \]

Question: An object is moving in the clockwise direction around the unit circle x<sup>2</sup> + y<sup>2</sup> = 1. As it passes through the point \[\left( {\frac{1}{2},\;\frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2}} \right),\]its y-coordinate is decreasing at the rate of 3 unit per second. So, $\sqrt 3 $ times the rate at which the x-coordinate changes at this point is ___ (in unit per second).
Solution:
\[{x^2} + {y^2} = 1\]
\[\begin{gathered}
   \Rightarrow 2x\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} + 2y\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = 0 \\
   \Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = \frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2}.3\left( {as\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = - 3} \right) \\
\end{gathered} \]
\[\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = 3\sqrt 3 \cdot \sqrt3\]

Rate at which the x-coordinate changes = 9 units per second

Question: The height of a cylinder is equal to the radius. If an error of \[\alpha \% \] is made in the height, then percentage error in its volume is
Solution:
\[h = r\] ..(i)
\[\frac{{\Delta h}}{h} \times 100 = \alpha \]
\[v = \pi {r^2}h\]
\[v = \pi {h^3}\] (using (i))
\[h = r\]
\[\frac{{dv}}{{dh}} = 3\pi {h^2}\]
so, \[\frac{{\Delta v}}{{\Delta h}} = 3\pi {h^2}\]
\[ \Rightarrow \frac{{\Delta v}}{v} \times 100 = \frac{{3\pi {h^2}.\Delta h}}{{\pi {h^3}}} \times 100 = 3\frac{{\Delta h}}{v} \times 100 = 3\alpha \% \]

Question: The difference between the greatest and least values of the function, f (x) = cos x + \[\frac{1}{2}\] cos 2x – \[\frac{1}{2}\]cos 3x is :
Solution:
\[f\left( x \right) = \cos x + {\cos ^2}x\frac{{ - 1}}{2}\frac{1}{3}\left[ {4{{\cos }^3}x - 3\cos x} \right]\]
\[ = - \frac{{4{{\cos }^3}x}}{3} + {\cos ^2}x + 2\cos x\]
\[f'\left( x \right) = - \left( { - 4{{\cos }^2}x + 2\cos x + 2} \right)\sin x\]
\[ = 2\sin x\left( {2{{\cos }^2}x - \cos x - 1} \right)\]
\[ = 2\sin x2\left( {\cos x - 1} \right)\left( {\cos x + \frac{1}{2}} \right)\]
\[4\sin x\left( {\cos x - 1} \right)\left( {\cos x + \frac{1}{2}} \right)\]

\[f\left( 0 \right) = 1 + \frac{1}{2} - \frac{1}{3} = \frac{{6 + 3 - 2}}{6} = \frac{7}{6}\]
\[f\left( {\frac{{2\pi }}{3}} \right) = - \frac{1}{2} - \frac{1}{4} - \frac{1}{3} = \frac{{6 + 3 - 2}}{{ - 12}} = \frac{{ - 13}}{{12}}\]
\[f\left( \pi \right) = - 1 + \frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{3} = \frac{{ - 6 + 3 + 2}}{6} = \frac{{ - 1}}{6}\]
\[f\left( {\frac{{4\pi }}{3}} \right) = - \frac{1}{2} - \frac{1}{2} - \frac{1}{3} = \frac{{ - 4}}{3}\]
So, Max \[ = \frac{7}{6}\], Min \[ = \frac{{ - 13}}{{12}}\]
So, \[\frac{7}{6} + \frac{{13}}{{12}} = \frac{{14 + 13}}{{12}} = \frac{{27}}{{12}} = \frac{9}{4}\]

Tags.Subject: Math
Tags.Topic: global extreme values in an interval

Question: The position of a point in time ‘t’ is given by x = a + bt–ct<sup>2</sup>, y = at + bt<sup>2</sup>. Its acceleration at time ‘t’ is
Area of the greatest rectangle that can be inscribed in the ellipse \[\frac{{{x^2}}}{{{a^2}}} + \frac{{{y^2}}}{{{b^2}}} = 1\] is
Solution:
Any point on the ellipse
\[\frac{{{{\text{x}}^{\text{2}}}}}{{{{\text{a}}^{\text{2}}}}}{\text{ + }}\frac{{{{\text{y}}^{\text{2}}}}}{{{{\text{b}}^{\text{2}}}}}{\text{ = 1}}\]

is \[(a\,\cos \,\theta \,,\,b\,\sin \,\theta \,)\] so the area of rectangle insribed in the ellipse is given by
\[A\, = \,(\,2\,a\,\cos \theta )(\,2b\,\sin \,\theta \,)\]
\[\therefore \,A\, = 2\,{\text{ab}}\,{\text{sin}}\,{\text{2}}\theta \, \Rightarrow \frac{{dA}}{{d\theta }} = 4\,{\text{ab cos 2}}\,\theta \]
Now, for maximum area
\[\frac{{dA}}{{d\theta }} = 0 \Rightarrow \theta = \frac{\pi }{4}\,\,{\text{and}}\,{\left( {\frac{{{d^2}A}}{{d{\theta ^2}}}} \right)_{\theta = \frac{\pi }{4}}}\, = {\left( { - \,8ab\,\,\sin \,\,2\theta } \right)_{\theta = \frac{\pi }{4}}} = - 8ab\]
\[{\text{as}}\,\,\frac{{{d^2}A}}{{d{\theta ^2}}} < \,0\,.\,\,\,\therefore \,\,{\text{Area}}\,\,{\text{is}}\,\,{\text{maximum}}\,\,{\text{for}}\,\,\theta \, = \frac{\pi }{4}\,\]
\[\therefore \]sides of rectangle are \[\frac{{{\text{2a}}}}{{\sqrt {\text{2}} }}\,{\text{,}}\,\frac{{{\text{2b}}}}{{\sqrt {\text{2}} }}\]
Required area = \[{\text{2ab}}\].

Question: The product of the lengths of subtangent and subnormal at any point of a curve is

Look at the conical funnel whose vertical angle is 90°. Water is dripping out from it at uniform rate of 8 cm3/sec through a tiny hole at the vertex. The rate at which the slant height of the water \[\left( l \right)\]is decreasing, when the slant height of the water is 4 cm, is$\frac{{a\sqrt b }}{\pi }$cm/sec. Then $a{b^2} = $
(where $a,\,\,b$ are natural numbers)
Solution:
Let V be the volume of the water at time ‘t’.
Since, water is in conical form.
Hence, $V = \frac{1}{3}\pi {r^2}h\,,$ where $r$ and $h$ are the radius and height of the water, respectively.

Since, semi-vertical angle of cone is ${45^0}$
Hence, \[r = h = \frac{l}{{\sqrt 2 }}\]
Therefore, $V = \frac{1}{3}\pi {\left( {\frac{l}{{\sqrt 2 }}} \right)^2}\frac{l}{{\sqrt 2 }} = \frac{{\pi {l^3}}}{{6\sqrt 2 }}$
Differentiating with respect to t we get
$\frac{{dv}}{{dt}} = \frac{{3\pi }}{{6\sqrt 2 }}{l^2}.\frac{{dl}}{{dt}}$
Now we are given $\frac{{dv}}{{dt}} = - 8\,{\text{c}}{{\text{m}}^3}/\sec $
Therefore, $\frac{{dl}}{{dt}} = - \frac{{16\sqrt 2 }}{{\pi {l^2}}}$
When the slant height is 4 cm
\[\frac{{dl}}{{dt}} = - \frac{{\sqrt 2 }}{\pi }\,\,{\text{cm}}/\sec \]
So, Slant height of the water is decreasing at the rate of $\frac{{\sqrt 2 }}{\pi }\,\,{\text{cm}}/\sec $
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{a\sqrt b }}{\pi } = \frac{{\sqrt 2 }}{\pi }$
$ \Rightarrow \,\,a = 1,\,\,b = 2\,\, \Rightarrow \,\,a{b^2} = 4$

Question: The maximum value of $xy$ subject to $x + y = 8,$ is
Solution:
$x + y = 8;$
$\therefore y = 8 - x$ .....(i)
Now $f(x) = xy = x(8 - x) = 8x - {x^2}$,
 $\therefore f'(x) = 8 - 2x$
For maximum value of $f(x),\,f'(x) = 0$
$\therefore \,\,x = 4$ and $y = 4$
So, maximum value of $xy = 4 \times 4 = 16$.

Question: 10,000 small balls, each weighing 1 g, strike one square cm of area per second with a velocity 100 m/s in a normal direction and rebound with the same speed. The value of pressure on the surface will be :
Solution:
Given mass of 1 ball = 1g

\[ = \frac{1}{{1000}}\;kg\]

Change in momentum of

1 ball \[ \Rightarrow \]

\[\Delta \vec p = m(100) - ( - m(100)]\]

= \[2m\left( {100} \right)\]

\[ = 2\left( {\frac{1}{{1000}}} \right)\;(100)\]

\[\Delta p\;of\;1\;ball\; = \frac{1}{5}kg\;m/s\]

\[ \Rightarrow \;\Delta p\;of\;10,000\;balls\;per\;{\text{second = 10,000}}\left( {\frac{1}{5}} \right)\]

= 2000 kg m/s2

\[\therefore \] \[1c{m^2}\,Area = 2000kg\,\,m/{s^2}\]

\[{\text{Pressure}} = \frac{{{\text{Normal}}\;{\text{force}}}}{{{\text{Area}}}}\]

\[{\text{Pressure}}\,\,{\text{ = }}\frac{{2000}}{{\left( {{{10}^{ - 4}}} \right)}}\]

\[ = 2 \times {10^7}N/{m^2}\]

Question: The length of subtangent to the curve x<sup>2</sup>y<sup>2</sup> = a<sup>4</sup> at the point (–a, a) is
Solution:
\[2x{y^2} + 2{x^2}y\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = 0\]
\[m = \frac{{ - y}}{x} = \frac{{ - a}}{{ - a}} = 1\]
So, Sub tangent = a

Question: For the curve y = 3 sin \[\theta \] cos \[\theta \], \[x = {e^\theta }\sin \theta ,0 \leqslant \theta  \leqslant \pi ,\] the tangent is parallel to x-axis when \[\theta \] is:
Solution:
\[y = 3\sin \theta \cos \theta \]
\[\begin{gathered}
   \Rightarrow y = \frac{3}{2}\sin 2\theta \\
  \frac{{dy}}{{d\theta }} = \frac{3}{2}.2\cos 2\theta = 3\cos 2\theta \\
  x = {e^\theta }\sin \theta \\
  \frac{{dx}}{{d\theta }} = {e^0}\left( {\sin \theta + \cos \theta } \right) \\
  \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{3\cos 2\theta }}{{{e^\theta }\left( {\sin \theta + \cos \theta } \right)}} = 0 \\
   \Rightarrow \cos 2\theta = 0 \\
   \Rightarrow 2\theta = \frac{\pi }{2} \\
   \Rightarrow \theta = \frac{\pi }{4} \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question: The two tangents to the curve ax<sup>2</sup> + 2hxy + by<sup>2</sup> = 1, a > 0 at the points where it crosses x-axis, are
Solution:
cosec x- axis
put y = 0
\[\begin{gathered}
  a{x^2} + 2hx \times 0 + b{o^2} = 1 \\
  a{x^2} = 1 \\
\end{gathered} \]
\[x = \pm \frac{1}{{\sqrt a }}\]
\[pts\left( {\frac{1}{{\sqrt a }},0} \right),\left( {\frac{{ - 1}}{{\sqrt a }},0} \right)\]
\[2ax + 2h\left[ {y + x\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}} \right] + 2by\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = 0\]
\[ax + hx\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = 0\]
\[\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{ - a}}{h}\]

Question: The radius of the base of a cone is increasing at the rate of 3 cm/minute and the altitude is decreasing at the rate of 4 cm/minute. The rate of change of lateral surface when the radius = 7 cm and altitude = 24 cm, in cm<sup>2</sup>/min is R, then calculate $\frac{R}{\pi}$:
Solution:
\[\frac{{dr}}{{dt}} = 3\,\,cm/\min \]
\[\frac{{dh}}{{dt}} = - 4\,\,{\text{cm/min}}\]
\[s = \pi rl\]
\[s = \pi r\sqrt {{r^2} + {h^2}} \]
\[\frac{{ds}}{{dt}} = \pi \left[ {\sqrt {{r^2} + {h^2}} \frac{{dr}}{{dt}} + \frac{r}{{\sqrt {{r^2} + {h^2}} }}\left( {r\frac{{dr}}{{dt}} + h\frac{{dh}}{{dt}}} \right)} \right]\]

   = $\pi \left[ {25 \times 3 + \frac{7}{{25}}\left\{ {21 - 96} \right\}} \right]\left( {{\text{Taking}}\,r = 7,\,h = 24} \right)$  
   = $\pi \left[ {75 + \frac{7}{{25}}\left( { - 75} \right)} \right]$
   = $\pi \left[ {75 - 21} \right]$
   = $54\pi c{m^2}/\min$
$\therefore R=54$

Question: If f (x) is differentiable in the interval [2, 5], where f (2) \[ = \frac{1}{5}\] and f (5) \[ = \frac{1}{2}\], then there exists a number c, 2 < c < 5 for which f ´ (c) is equal to
Solution:
\[f'\left( c \right) = \frac{{f\left( 5 \right) - f\left( {\frac{1}{2}} \right)}}{{5 - 2}}\] (By LMVT)
\[ = \frac{{\frac{1}{2} - \frac{1}{5}}}{3} = \frac{3}{{10}} \times \frac{1}{3} = \frac{1}{{10}}\]

Question: The maximum value of the function y = x (x –1)<sup>2</sup>, 0 \[ \leqslant \] x \[ \leqslant \] 2 is
Solution:
\[\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = {\left( {x - 1} \right)^2} + 2x\left( {x - 1} \right)\]
\[ = \left( {x - 1} \right)\left( {x - 1 + 2x} \right)\]
\[ = 3\left( {x - 1} \right)\left( {x - \frac{1}{3}} \right)\]

\[\begin{gathered}
  f\left( {\frac{1}{3}} \right) = \frac{1}{3} \times \frac{4}{9} = \frac{4}{{27}} \\
  f\left( 2 \right) = 2 \\
\end{gathered} \]
So mixmum value is 2.

Question: For what values of x is the rate of increase of x<sup>3</sup> – 5x<sup>2</sup> + 5x + 8 is twice the rate of increase of x ?
The tangent to the curve x<sup>2</sup> + y<sup>2</sup> = 25 is parallel to the line 3x – 4y = 7 at the point
Solution:
\[2x + 2y\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = 0\]
\[\frac{{dy}}{{dx}} = \frac{{ - x}}{y} = \frac{3}{4}\] (As tangent is parallel to the line 3x – 4y = 7)
\[y = \frac{{ - 4x}}{3}\]
So, \[{x^2} + \frac{{16}}{9}{x^2} = 25\]
\[\frac{{25{x^2}}}{9} = 25\]
\[x = 3\] or \[ - 3\]
So , Points are \[\left( {3, - 4} \right)\left( { - 3,4} \right)\]

Question: The function f (x) = x<sup>2</sup> (x –2)<sup>2</sup>
Solution:
\[f'\left( x \right) = 2x{\left( {x - 2} \right)^2} + 2{x^2}\left( {x - 2} \right)\]
\[ = 2x\left( {x - 2} \right)\left( {x - 2 + x} \right)\]
\[ = 4x\left( {x - 1} \right)\left( {x - 2} \right)\]

\[\begin{gathered}
  f\left( 0 \right) = 0 \\
  f\left( 1 \right) = 1 \\
  f\left( 2 \right) = 0 \\
\end{gathered} \]
so local maximum value at x = 1 as f’(x) changes from +ve to –ve.

Question: The greatest and the least value of the function, f (x) = \[\sqrt {{\text{1}}\,{\text{-}}\,{\text{2x}}\,{\text{ + }}\,{{\text{x}}^{\text{2}}}} \,\,-\,\,\sqrt {{\text{1}}\,{\text{ + }}\,{\text{2x}}\,{\text{ + }}\,{{\text{x}}^{\text{2}}}} \], \[x \in \left( { - \infty ,\infty } \right)\]are
Solution:
\[f'\left( x \right) = \left| {x - 1} \right| - \left| {x + 1} \right|\]
\[ = \left\{ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  { - 2}&;&{x \geqslant 1} \\
  { - 2x}&;&{ - 1 \leqslant x < 1} \\
  2&;&{x < - 1}
\end{array}} \right.\]

From graph, we can say that greatest value = 2 and least value = –2.

Question: The function f (x) = 2x<sup>2</sup> – log | x | monotonically decreases for
Solution:
\[f'\left( x \right) \leqslant 0\]
\[4x - \frac{1}{x} \leqslant 0\]
\[\frac{{4{x^2} - 1}}{x} \leqslant 0 \Rightarrow \frac{{\left( {2x - 1} \right)\left( {2x + 1} \right)}}{x} \leqslant 0\]
\[\frac{{\left( {x + \frac{1}{2}} \right)\left( {x - \frac{1}{2}} \right)}}{x} \leqslant 0\]

So, \[x \in \left( { - \infty , - \frac{1}{2}} \right] \cup \left( {0,\frac{1}{2}} \right]\]

Question: If \[f(x) = \frac{1}{{x + 1}}\]– log (1 + x), x > 0, then f is
Solution:
\[f'\left( x \right) = \frac{{ - 1}}{{{{\left( {x + 1} \right)}^2}}} - \frac{1}{{1 + x}}\]
\[ = \frac{{ - 1}}{{1 + x}}\left[ {\frac{1}{{x + 1}} + 1} \right]\]
\[ = \frac{{ - \left( {x + 2} \right)}}{{{{\left( {x + 1} \right)}^2}}}\]
\[\because \;x > 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \frac{{x + 2}}{{{{\left( {x + 1} \right)}^2}}} > 0\]
\[ = \frac{{ - \left( {x + 2} \right)}}{{{{\left( {x + 1} \right)}^2}}} < 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow f'\left( x \right) < 0\]

So, \[f'\left( x \right) < 0\] \[\forall \times > 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \]f is a decreasing function.

Question: Let AD and BC be two vertical poles at A and B respectively on a horizontal ground. If AD = 8m, BC = 11m and AB = 10m; then the distance (in meters) of a point M on AB from the point A such that MD<sup>2</sup> + MC<sup>2</sup> is minimum is _____.
Solution:
\[{\left( {MD} \right)^2} = {x^2} + {8^2} = {x^2} + 64\]
\[{\left( {MC} \right)^2} = {\left( {10 - x} \right)^2} + {\left( {11} \right)^2} = {\left( {x - 10} \right)^2} + 121\]
\[f(x) = {\left( {MD} \right)^2} + {\left( {MC} \right)^2} = {x^2} + 64 + {\left( {x - 10} \right)^2} + \left( {121} \right)\]
\[f'(x) = 0\]
\[2x + 2\left( {x - 10} \right) = 0\]
\[4x = 20 \Rightarrow x = 5\]
And in this case  \[f''(x) = 4 > 0\]
\[\therefore \] $x = 5$ is point of minima.

Question: If the tangent at each point of the curve y = \[\frac{2}{3}\] x<sup>3</sup> – 2ax<sup>2</sup> + 2x + 5 makes an acute angle with the positive direction of x-axis, then

Question: If the distance ‘s’ metres travelled by a particle in t seconds is given by s = t<sup>3</sup> – 3t<sup>2</sup>, then the velocity of the particle when the acceleration is zero in m/s is
Gas is being pumped into a spherical balloon at the rate of 30 ft<sup>3</sup>/min. Then, the rate at which the radius increases when it reaches the value 15 ft, is

Question: If \[V = \frac{4}{3}\pi {r^3},\] at what rate in cubic units is V increasing when r = 10 and  \[\frac{{dr}}{{dt}} = 0.01\]?
Solution:
\[\frac{{dv}}{{dt}} = 4\pi {r^2}\frac{{dr}}{{dt}}\]
\[ = 4\pi \times 100 \times 0.01\]
\[ = 4\pi \]

Question: The angle at which the curves y = sin x and y = cos x intersect in [0, \[\pi \]], is
Solution:
\[\tan x = 1\]
\[x = \frac{\pi }{4}\] or \[x = \frac{{5\pi }}{4}\]
So, \[{\left. {\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}} \right)_1} = cosx\] and \[{\left. {\frac{{dy}}{{dx}}} \right)_2} = - \sin x\]
For \[x = \frac{\pi }{4}\]:
\[\tan \theta = \frac{{2 \times \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}}}{{1 - \frac{1}{2}}} = 2\sqrt 2 \]
\[\theta = {\tan ^{ - 1}}2\sqrt 2 \]
For \[x = \frac{{5\pi }}{4}\]:
\[\tan \theta = 2\sqrt 2 \]
\[\theta = {\tan ^{ - 1}}2\sqrt 2 \]

Question: The interval in which the function x<sup>3</sup> increases less rapidly than 6x<sup>2</sup> + 15x + 5 is :

<!-StartFragment->If $f\left( x \right) = \frac{{3{x^2} + 1}}{{\sqrt {{x^4} + {x^2}} }}\forall \,\,0 < x \leqslant \sqrt 2 $, then $f\left( x \right)$ has minimum value $M$ at $x = m$. Then, $\left( {{M^2} + \frac{1}{{{m^2}}}} \right)$ is equal to<!-EndFragment->
Solution:
$\because \frac{{2x}}{{\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} }} + \frac{{\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} }}{x} \geqslant 2\sqrt {\frac{{2x}}{{\sqrt {{x^2}} + 1}} \times \frac{{\sqrt {{x^2} + 1} }}{x}} = 2\sqrt 2 $
$\therefore M = 2\sqrt 2 \& m = \pm 1,\,\,$i.e., $m = 1$

Question: Let v<sub>1</sub> be the frequency of the series limit of the Lyman series, v<sub>2</sub> be the frequency of the first line of the Lyman series, and v<sub>3</sub> be the frequency of the series limit of the Balmer series.
Solution:
Series Limit frequency is the frequency needed to transit electron out from atom
So, ${n_2} = \infty $
So, $v \propto \left( {\frac{1}{{n_1^2}} - \frac{1}{{n_2^2}}} \right)$
So, ${v_1} = \frac{1}{{{1^2}}}$
${v_2} = \frac{1}{{{1^2}}} - \frac{1}{{{2^2}}}$
${v_3} = \frac{1}{{{2^2}}} = {v_1} - {v_2}$

Question: An alpha nucleus of energy $\frac{1}{2}m{v^2}$ bombards a heavy nuclear target of charge Ze. Then the distance of closest approach for the alpha nucleus will be proportional to
Solution:
When the particle is closest its KE = 0
Let distance of closest approach be r
Applying conservation of energy
$K{E_i} + P{E_i} = K{E_f} + P{E_f}$
$\frac{1}{2}m{v^2} + \frac{{K\left( {2e} \right)\left( {Ze} \right)}}{\infty }$
$ = 0 + \frac{{K\left( {2e} \right)\left( {Ze} \right)}}{r}$
$\frac{1}{2}m{v^2} = \frac{{K\left( {2e} \right)\left( {Ze} \right)}}{r}$
$r = \frac{{4kZ{e^2}}}{{m{v^2}}}$
$r \propto \frac{1}{m}$

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Topic: Electric potential energy of a system of charges

Question: The wavelengths of K<sub>α </sub> X–rays of two metals 'A' and 'B' are $\frac{4}{{1875R}}$ and $\frac{1}{{675R}}$ respectively, where 'R' is Rydberg constant. The number of elements lying between 'A' and 'B' according to their atomic numbers is :
Solution:
$\frac{1}{\lambda } = R{\left( {Z - 1} \right)^2}\left[ {\frac{1}{{n_1^2}} - \frac{1}{{n_2^2}}} \right]$
For wavelength of ${K_\alpha },\,{n_1} = 1\,\,to\,\,{n_2} = 2$
$\frac{{1875R}}{4} = R{\left( {{Z_A} - 1} \right)^2}\left( {1 - \frac{1}{4}} \right)$ ….(i)
And $\frac{{675R}}{1} = R{\left[ {{Z_B} - 1} \right]^2}\left[ {1 - \frac{1}{4}} \right]$ ….(ii)
By solving eq. (i) & (ii) we get
${Z_A} = 26$ and ${Z_B} = 31$
[Four elements lie between these two]

Question: The radiation emitted when an electron jumps from n = 4 to n = 3 in a Li<sup>++</sup> ion (atomic no. z = 3) falls on a metal surface to produce photoelectron. When the photoelectron with maximum kinetic energy are made to move perpendicular to a uniform magnetic field of $$2{\text{ }} \times {\text{ }}{10^{ - 4}}\;T$$, it follows a circular path of radius $\sqrt {\frac{{9.1}}{{1.6}}\,} {\text{cm}}{\text{.}}$ The work function of metal (in eV) to the nearest integer is (give mass of electron $$m{\text{ }} = {\text{ }}9.1{\text{ }} \times {\text{ }}{10^{ - 31}}\;kg$$ and charge on electron $$ = {\text{ }} - 1.6{\text{ }} \times {\text{ }}{10^{ - 19}}\;C$$)
Solution:
$\begin{gathered}
  r = \frac{{mv}}{{qB}} = \sqrt {\frac{{2m{K_{\max }}}}{{qB}}} \\
   \Rightarrow \,\,{K_{\max }} = \frac{{{q^2}{B^2}{r^2}}}{{2m}} \\
\end{gathered} $
Putting the values of q, B, r and m
${K_{\max }} = 2eV$
Energy of emitted photon
$\begin{gathered}
  hv = 13.6 \times {3^2}\left( {\frac{1}{9} - \frac{1}{{16}}} \right)eV = 5.95\,eV \\
  hv = \phi + {k_{\max }} \\
   \Rightarrow \,\,\phi = 3.95\,eV \approx ~4eV \\
\end{gathered} $

Question: An alpha nucleus of energy $\frac{1}{2}m{v^2}$ bombards a heavy nuclear target of change Ze. Then the distance of closest approach for the alpha nucleus will be proportional to
Solution:
For Closest approach, kinetic energy is converted into potential energy
$\therefore \frac{1}{2}m{v^2} = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\,\,\frac{{{q_1}{q_2}}}{{{r_0}}} = \frac{1}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}}}\,\,\frac{{\left( {Ze} \right)\left( {2e} \right)}}{{{r_0}}}$
Or ${r_0} = \frac{{4z{e^2}}}{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}m{v^2}}} = \frac{{Z{e^2}}}{{\pi {\varepsilon _o}{v^2}}}\left( {\frac{1}{m}} \right)$
Or ${r_0}$ is proportional to $\left( {\frac{1}{m}} \right)$

Question: Which of the following atoms has the lowest ionization potential ?
Solution: ${}_{55}^{144}Cs$ has the lowest ionization potential. Of the four atoms given, Cs has the largest size. Electrons in the outer most orbit are at large distance from nucleus in a large-size atom. Hence the ionization potential is the least.

Question: Taking the wavelength of first Balmer line in hydrogen spectrum (n = 3 to n = 2) as 660 nm, the wavelength of the 2<sup>nd</sup> Balmer line (n = 4 to n = 2) will be (in nm, as nearest integer);
Solution:
$\begin{gathered}
  \frac{1}{{{\lambda _1}}} = R\left( {\frac{1}{{{2^2}}} - \frac{1}{{{3^2}}}} \right) = \frac{{5R}}{{36}} \\
  \frac{1}{{{\lambda _2}}} = R\left( {\frac{1}{{{2^2}}} - \frac{1}{{{4^2}}}} \right) = \frac{{3R}}{{16}} \\
  \therefore \frac{{{\lambda _2}}}{{{\lambda _1}}} = \frac{{80}}{{108}} \\
  {\lambda _2} = \frac{{80}}{{108}}{\lambda _1} = \frac{{80}}{{108}} \times 660 = 488.9\,{\text{nm}}. \\
\end{gathered} $

Question: A hydrogen like atom (atomic number Z) is in a higher state of quantum number n. Question: The wavelength of the first line of Lyman series of hydrogen is 1216 Å. The wavelength of the second line of the same series will be
Solution:
$\frac{{{h_c}}}{{{\lambda _1}}} = 13.6\left[ {\frac{1}{{{1^1}}} - \frac{1}{{{2^2}}}} \right] = 13.6 \times \frac{3}{4}$
$\frac{{{h_c}}}{{{\lambda _2}}} = 13.6\left[ {\frac{1}{{{1^2}}} - \frac{1}{{{3^2}}}} \right] = 13.6\, \times \frac{8}{9}$
$ \Rightarrow \frac{{{\lambda _2}}}{{{\lambda _1}}} = \frac{3}{4} \times \frac{9}{8} \Rightarrow {\lambda _2} = \frac{{1026\mathop A\limits^ \circ }}{{\left( {{\lambda _1} = 1216\mathop A\limits^ \circ } \right)}}$

Question: Hydrogen (<sub>1</sub>H<sup>1</sup>), Deuterium (<sub>1</sub>H<sup>2</sup>), singly ionised Helium (<sub>2</sub>He<sup>4</sup>)<sup>+</sup> and doubly ionised lithium (<sub>3</sub>Li<sup>6</sup>)<sup>+ +</sup> all have one electron around the nucleus. Consider an electron transition from n = 2 to n = 1. If the wave lengths of emitted radiation are λ <sub>1</sub>, λ <sub>2</sub>, λ <sub>3</sub>, and λ <sub>4</sub> respectively then approximately which one of the following is correct ?
Solution:
For a given transition ${n_2} \to {n_1}$ the wavelength emitted can be given as :
$\frac{1}{\lambda } = R{Z^2}\left[ {\frac{1}{{n_1^2}} - \frac{1}{{n_2^2}}} \right]$
As ${n_2} = 2$ and ${n_1} = 1$ for all
$ \Rightarrow \lambda \propto \frac{1}{{{Z^2}}}$
For $_1{H^1}$ and $_1{H^2}$ we take $Z = 1$
$ \Rightarrow {\lambda _1} \cong {\lambda _2}$
And for ${\left( {_2H{e^4}} \right)^ + }Z = 2$
$ \Rightarrow {\lambda _3} = \frac{{{\lambda _1}}}{4}$
And for ${\left( {{}_3L{e^6}} \right)^{ + 2}}Z = 3$
$ \Rightarrow {\lambda _4} = \frac{{{\lambda _1}}}{9}$
$ \Rightarrow {\lambda _1} \equiv {\lambda _2} \equiv 4{\lambda _3} \equiv 9{\lambda _2}$

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Topic: Energy, radius and speed of electron in hydrogen like elements

Question: The binding energy per nucleon of deuteron $\left( {{}_2^4H} \right)$ and helium nucleus $\left( {{}_2^4He} \right)$ is 1.1 MeV and 7 MeV respectively. If two deuteron nuclei react to form a single helium, nucleus then the energy released is
Solution:
Total binding energy for (each deuteron)
$ = 2 \times 1.1 = 2.2\,MeV$
Total binding energy for helium = 4 7 28 MeV
∴ Energy released = 28 – (2 × 2.2)
= 28 – 4.4 = 23.6 MeV

Question: The ratio of radius of nucleus $_{13}A{l^{27}}$to that of $_{52}{X^A}$ is 3: 5. The number of neutrons in the nuclei of X will be:
Solution:
$r \propto {A^{1/3}}$ ⇒ $\frac{{{r_1}}}{{{r_2}}} = {\left( {\frac{{{A_1}}}{{{A_2}}}} \right)^{1/3}}$
⇒ $\frac{3}{5} = {\left( {\frac{{27}}{A}} \right)^{1/3}}$⇒ $\frac{{27}}{{125}} = \frac{{27}}{A}$⇒ $A = 125$

Number of nuclei in atom X $ = A - 52 = 125 - 52 = 73.$

Question: A hydrogen atom emits a photon corresponding to an electron transition from n = 5 to n = 1. The recoil speed of hydrogen atom is almost
Solution:
During transition from n = 5 to n = 1 Energy of photon released
$ = \left( {\frac{{ - 13.6}}{{{5^2}}}} \right) - \left( {\frac{{ - 13.6}}{{{1^2}}}} \right)$
$ = - 13.6 \times \frac{{24}}{{25}}eV$
so, by principle of conservation of linear momentum.
$\left( {{P_{photon}} = \frac{E}{C}} \right) \Rightarrow \left| {\overrightarrow p \,photon} \right| = \left| {\overrightarrow P \,Hyd.atom} \right|$

$\left( {Photon = \frac{E}{C}} \right) \Rightarrow \frac{{13.6 \times \frac{{24}}{{25}} \times 1.6 \times {{10}^{ - 19}}}}{{3 \times {{10}^8}}} = m \times v$
$v \approx 4m/s$

Question: Energy levels A, B, C of a certain atom correspond to increasing values of energy i.e., EA < EB < EC. If λ 1, λ 2, λ 3, are the wavelengths of radiations corresponding to the transitions C to B, B to A and C to A respectively, which of the following statements is correct ?
Solution:
According to given diagram,
$\frac{{hC}}{{{\lambda _1}}} = {E_c} - {E_B}$ …..(1)
$\frac{{hC}}{{{\lambda _2}}} = {E_B} - {E_A}$ ……(2)
And $\frac{{hc}}{{{\lambda _3}}} = {E_c} - {E_A}$ …..(3)
So equation
(1) + (2) = Eq. 3
$ \Rightarrow \frac{1}{{{\lambda _1}}} + \frac{1}{{{\lambda _2}}} = \frac{1}{{{\lambda _3}}}$
${\lambda _3} = \frac{{{\lambda _1}{\lambda _2}}}{{{\lambda _1} + {\lambda _2}}}$

Question: Suppose an electron is attracted towards the origin by a force k/r, where k is a constant and r is the distance of the electron from the origin. By applying Bohr model to this system, the radius of the n<sup>th</sup> orbital of the electron is found to be r<sub>n</sub> and the kinetic energy of the electron to be T<sub>n</sub>. Then which of the following is true.
Solution:
We know that the force on the electron is centripetal in nature.
$f = \frac{K}{r} = \frac{{m{v^2}}}{r}$
$ \Rightarrow v = \frac{{\sqrt k }}{m}$
Now, from Bohr’s model its angular momentum is given by,
$L = mvr = \frac{{nh}}{{2\pi }}$
$ \Rightarrow r = \frac{{nh}}{{2\pi mv}}as\,\,v$ is constant.
$ \Rightarrow r \propto n$
For T
$T = \frac{1}{2}m{v^2}$
But v is independent of n
$ \Rightarrow $ T is independent of n

Tags.Topic: Energy, radius and speed of electron in hydrogen like elements

Question: If an electron has, orbital angular momentum quantum number $l = 7$, then it will have an orbital angular momentum equal to
Solution:
If angular momentum quantum Number = 7
$ \Rightarrow n = \sqrt {7\left( {7 + 1} \right)} = \sqrt {56} $
So, $L = \frac{{nh}}{{2\pi }} = \sqrt {56} \frac{h}{{2\pi }}$

Question: The wavelength of K<sub>α </sub> line for an element of atomic number 43 is λ . Then the wavelength of K<sub>α </sub> line for an element of atomic number 29 is:-
Solution:
$\frac{1}{\lambda } = R{\left( {Z - 1} \right)^2}\left[ {\frac{1}{{n_1^2}} - \frac{1}{{n_2^2}}} \right]$
For ${K_a}$ line, ${n_1} = 1,\,\,{n_2} = 2$
$\frac{1}{\lambda } = R{\left( {43 - 1} \right)^2}\left[ {\frac{1}{{{1^2}}} - \frac{1}{{{2^2}}}} \right] = R{\left( {42} \right)^2}\left( {\frac{3}{4}} \right)$….(i)
And $\frac{1}{{\lambda '}} = R\left( {29 - 1} \right)\left[ {\frac{1}{{{1^2}}} - \frac{1}{{{2^2}}}} \right] = R{\left( {28} \right)^2}\left( {\frac{3}{4}} \right)$….(ii)
Dividing eq. (i) by (ii), we get $\frac{{\lambda '}}{\lambda } = \frac{9}{4} \Rightarrow \lambda ' = \frac{9}{4}\lambda $

Question: The slopes of the isothermal and adiabatic curves are related as:
Options: isothermal curve slope = adiabatic curve slope
isothermal curve slope = $\gamma $ × adiabatic curve slope
adiabatic curve slope = $\gamma $ × isothermal curve slope
adiabatic curve slope = (1/2) × isothermal curve slope
Solution:
For Isothermal
PV = Constant

\[ \Rightarrow {\text{dPV}} + {\text{PdV}} = 0 \Rightarrow \frac{{{\text{dP}}}}{{{\text{dV}}}} = \frac{{-{\text{P}}}}{{\text{V}}}\]

For adiabatic

\[P{V^\gamma } = {\text{Constant}}\]

\[dP\,.\,{V^\gamma } + P\gamma \;dV\,.\,{V^{\gamma - 1}} = {\text{constant}}\] \[\frac{{dP}}{{dV}} = - \gamma \frac{P}{V}\]

Hence, adiabatic curve slope = $\gamma \times $ isothermal curve slope

Question: In a hydrogen like atom electron makes transition from an energy level with quantum number n to another with quantum number (n – 1). If n >> 1, the frequency of radiation emitted is proportional to :
Solution:
In a hydrogen like atom, when an electron makes an transition from an energy level with n to n –1, the frequency of emitted radiation is
$f = \operatorname{Re} {Z^2}\left[ {\frac{1}{{{{\left( {n - 1} \right)}^2}}} - \frac{1}{{{n^2}}}} \right]$
\[ = \operatorname{Re} {Z^2}\left[ {\frac{{{n^2} - {{\left( {n - 1} \right)}^2}}}{{\left( {{n^2}} \right){{\left( {n - 1} \right)}^2}}}} \right] = \frac{{\operatorname{Re} {Z^2}\left( {2n - 1} \right)}}{{{n^2}{{\left( {n - 1} \right)}^2}}}\]
As n > >1
$f = \frac{{\operatorname{Re} {Z^2}2n}}{{{n^4}}} = \frac{{2\operatorname{Re} {Z^4}}}{{{n^3}}}or\,\,f \propto \frac{1}{{{n^3}}}$

Question: An electron in the ground state of hydrogen has an angular momentum L<sub>1</sub>, and an electron in the first excited state of lithium has an angular momentum L<sub>2</sub>

Question: The roots of the equation |x<sup>2</sup> – x – 6| = x + 2 are
Solution:
\[\left| {{x^2} - x - 6} \right| = \left| {\left( {x - 3} \right)\left( {x + 2} \right)} \right|\]
By wavy curve method

\[so\,\left| {\left( {x - 3} \right)\left( {x + 2} \right)} \right| = \left\{ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\left( {x - 3} \right)\left( {x + 2} \right)\,{\text{when}}\,x \leqslant - 2\,\left( {or} \right)\,x \geqslant 3} \\
  { - \left( {x - 3} \right)\left( {x + 2} \right)\,{\text{when}}\, - 2 < x < 3}
\end{array}} \right.\]
Case - 1 : when \[x \leqslant - 1\,\,or\,\,x \geqslant 3\] then given equation becomes
\[\left( {x - 3} \right)\left( {x + 2} \right) = x + 2\]
\[ \Rightarrow \left( {x - 3} \right)\left( {x + 2} \right) - \left( {x + 2} \right) = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \left( {x + 2} \right)\left( {x - 3 - 1} \right) = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \left( {x + 2} \right)\left( {x - 4} \right) = 0\]
x = -2, 4
Case -2 : when -2 < x < 3 then given equation becomes
-(x – 3) (x + 2) = x + 2
\[ \Rightarrow \, - \left( {x - 3} \right)\left( {x + 2} \right) - \left( {x + 2} \right) = 0\]
(x + 2) (-x + 2) = 0
x = -2, 2
x = 2 is possible because –2 < x < 3
so from case 1 and case 2 roots are -2, 2, 4

Question: If  x<sup>2</sup> – 1 ≤ 0 and  x<sup>2</sup> – x – 2 ≥ 0, then x lies in the interval set
Solution:
\[{x^2} - 1 \leqslant 0\,and\,{x^2} - x - 2 \geqslant 0\]
\[(x + 1)(x - 1) \leqslant 0\,and\,(x - 2)(x + 1) \geqslant 0\]
\[x \in [ - 1,1]\,and\,x \in \left( { - \infty , - 1} \right] \cup \left[ {2,\infty } \right)\]
\[ \Rightarrow x \in \{ - 1\} \]

Question: Let \[\operatorname{f} {\text{(x)}} = \operatorname{l} {\text{o}}{{\text{g}}_{{x^2}}}25\] and g(x) = log<sub>x</sub> 5 then f (x) = g(x) holds for x belonging to
Solution:
\[f(x) = {\log _{{x^2}}}25,\,\,\,\,\,g(x) = {\log _x}5\]
clearly \[x > 0\,\,\,and\,\,\,x \ne 1\]
\[ \Rightarrow {\log _{{x^2}}}25 = {\log _x}5\]
\[ \Rightarrow {\log _{{x^2}}}{(5)^2} = {\log _x}5\]
\[ \Rightarrow {\log _x}5 = {\log _x}5\]
\[ \Rightarrow x \in (0,1) \cup (1,\infty )\]

Question: The solution set of \[\frac{{{x^2} - 3x + 4}}{{x + 1}} > 1,\,\,x\,\, \in \,\,\,R\] is
Solution:
\[\frac{{{x^2} - 3x + 4}}{{x + 1}} > 1\]
\[ \Rightarrow \frac{{{x^2} - 3x + 4 - x - 1}}{{x + 1}} > 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \frac{{{x^2} - 4x + 3}}{{x + 1}} > 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \frac{{(x - 1)(x - 3)}}{{x + 1}} > 0\]

\[ \Rightarrow x \in ( - 1,1) \cup (3,\infty )\]

Question: Find the set of values of 'x' for which the given condition is true (x – 1) (x – 3) (x + 5) > 0
Solution:
\[(x - 1)(x - 3)(x + 5) > 0\]

\[ \Rightarrow x \in ( - 5,1) \cup (3,\infty )\]

Question: The number of real solution of the equations x<sup>2</sup> – 3|x| + 2 = 0 is
Solution:
Given \[{x^2} - 3\left| x \right| + 2 = 0\]
If \[x \geqslant 0\] i.e., \[\left| x \right| = x\]
∴ then given equation can be written as
\[{x^2} - 3x + 2 = 0 \Rightarrow \left( {x - 1} \right)\left( {x - 2} \right) = 0 \Rightarrow x = 1,2\]
similarly for x < 0, \[{x^2} - 3\left| x \right| + 2 = 0\]
\[{x^2} + 3x + 2 = 0 \Rightarrow x = - 1, - 2\]
Hence 1, –1, 2, –2 are four solutions of the given equation.

Question: If the value for which \[\frac{{\left( {x - 1} \right)}}{x}\,\, \geqslant \,\,2\] is [–k, 0), then the value of k is
Solution:
\[\frac{{x - 1}}{x} \geqslant 2\]
\[ \Rightarrow \frac{{x - 1}}{x} - 2 \geqslant 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \frac{{x - 1 - 2x}}{x} \geqslant 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \frac{{ - (1 + x)}}{x} \geqslant 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \frac{{1 + x}}{x} \leqslant 0\]
⇒ 
\[ \Rightarrow x \in \left[ { - 1,0} \right)\,\]

Question: If a ∈ R and the equation –3 (x – [x])<sup>2</sup> + 2 (x – [x]) + a<sup>2</sup> = 0 (where [x] denotes the greatest integer ≤ x) has no integral solution, then all possible values of a lie in the interval:
Solution:
Let {x} = t so \[0 \leqslant t < 1\], we have
\[3t_2^{} - 2t - {a^2} = 0\]
D = 4 + 12a2 > 0
Then the equation has discriminant as positive. Assume that the roots are between 0 and 1, we have to ensure that there is no integral roots, i.e., \[t \ne 0\]
When \[t = 0 \Rightarrow {a^2} = 0\]
∴ a = 0
Product of roots = \[\frac{{{a^2}}}{3} < 0\]
Thus one root is positive and the other is negative. The condition that the root is strictly between 0 and 1 is

\[f\left( 0 \right)f\left( 1 \right) < 0\,\,\, \Rightarrow - {a^2}\left( {1 - {a^2}} \right) < 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow {a^2} - 1 < 0\,\, \Rightarrow {a^2} < 1\,\,\,\therefore a \in \left( { - 1,1} \right)\]
For integral roots, a = 0
∴ The set of all possible values of a is \[\left( { - 1,0} \right) \cup \left( {0,1} \right)\]

Question: |2x – 3| < |x + 5|, then x belongs to
Solution:
\[\left| {2x - 3} \right| < \left| {x + 5} \right|\]
Square both sides
\[4{x^2} + 9 - 12x < {x^2} + 10x + 25\]
\[ \Rightarrow 3{x^2} - 22x - 16 < 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow 3{x^2} - 24x + 2x - 16 < 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow 3x(x - 8) + 2(x - 8) < 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow x \in \left( {\frac{{ - 2}}{3},8} \right)\]

Question: The set of values of x which satisfy the inequations 5x + 2 < 3x + 8 and \[\frac{{x + 2}}{{x - 1}}\; < 4\] is
Solution:
\[5x + 2 < 3x + 8\,and\,\frac{{x + 2}}{{x - 1}} < 4\]
\[2x < 6\,and\,\frac{{x + 2 - 4x + 4}}{{x - 1}} < 0\]
\[x < 3\,and\,\frac{{6 - 3x}}{{x - 1}} < 0\]
\[x < 3\,and\,\frac{{x - 2}}{{x - 1}} > 0\]

\[ \Rightarrow x \in ( - \infty ,1) \cup (2,3)\]

Question: The value of x for which 12 x – 6 < 0 and 12 – 3 x < 0
Solution:
\[12x - 6 < 0\,and\,12 - 3x < 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow x < \frac{1}{2}\,\,and\,x > 4\]
\[ \Rightarrow x \in \phi \]

Question: If \[\frac{1}{a}\,\, < \,\,\frac{1}{b}\], then :
Solution:
\[\frac{1}{a} < \frac{1}{b} \Rightarrow a > b\,\,\,(if\,ab > 0)\]
\[\frac{1}{a} < \frac{1}{b} \Rightarrow a < b\,\,\,\,(if\,ab < 0)\]

Question: The sum of all real roots of the equation |x – 2|<sup>2</sup> + |x – 2| – 2 = 0, is
Solution:
\[\left| {x - 2} \right| = \left\{ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\left( {x - 2} \right)when\,x \geqslant 2} \\
  { - \left( {x - 2} \right)when\,x < 2}
\end{array}} \right.\]
Case - 1 : when\[x \geqslant 2\] then given equation becomes.
\[{\left( {x - 2} \right)^2} + \left( {x - 2} \right) - 2 = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow {x^2} - 3x = 0\]
x = 0, 3 only x = 3 is valid because \[x \geqslant 2\]
Case - 2: when x < 2 then given equation becomes.
\[{\left( {x - 2} \right)^2} - \left( {x - 2} \right) - 2 = 0\]
\[{x^2} - 5x + 4 = 0 \Rightarrow x = 1,4\]
only x = 1 is valid because x < 2
so sum of roots 3 + 1 = 4

Question: The number of roots of the equation \[3{x^{{{\log }_5}2}} + {2^{{{\log }_5}x}} = 64\] is
Solution:
\[3{x^{{{\log }_5}2}} + {2^{{{\log }_5}x}} = 64\]
$ \Rightarrow 3{x^{{{\log }_5}2}} + {x^{{{\log }_5}2}} = 64\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left\{ {\because \,\,\,{a^{{{\log }_x}b}} = {b^{{{\log }_x}a}}} \right\}$
\[ \Rightarrow \;4{x^{{{\log }_5}2}} = 64\; \Rightarrow \;{x^{{{\log }_5}2}} = 16\]
\[ \Rightarrow \;{2^{{{\log }_5}x}} = {2^4} \Rightarrow {\log _5}x = 4\]
\[ \Rightarrow \;x = {5^4} = 625\]
∴ The number of roots is 1

Question: The equation x<sup>2</sup> –3 |x| + 2 = 0 has how many real roots
Solution:
\[\left| x \right| = \left\{ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {x\,when\,x \geqslant 0} \\
  { - x\,when\,x < 0}
\end{array}} \right.\]
Case - 1 : when \[x \geqslant 0\] then given equation becomes.
\[{x^2} - 3x + 2 = 0\]
\[\left( {x - 1} \right)\left( {x - 2} \right) = 0\]
x = 1, 2 both values are valid because \[x \geqslant 0\]
Case - 2: when x < 0 then given equation becomes.
\[{x^2} + 3x + 2 = 0\]
\[\left( {x + 1} \right)\left( {x + 2} \right) = 0\]
x = -1, -2 both values are valid because x < 0
so total roots are 4, that are real.

Question: The largest interval among the following for which x<sup>12</sup> – x<sup>9</sup> + x<sup>4</sup> – x + 1 > 0 is
Solution:
\[Case - I:\;x \leqslant 0\]
\[{x^{12}} \geqslant 0,\; - {x^9} \geqslant 0,\;{x^4} \geqslant 0,\; - x \geqslant 0,\;so\;LHS > 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \;{x^{12}} - {x^9} + {x^4} - x + 1 > 0\]
\[Case - II:\;0 < x \leqslant 1\]
\[{x^9} < {x^4}\;\& \;x < 1\; \Rightarrow \;{x^{12}} - {x^9} + {x^4} - x + 1 > 0\]
\[Case - III:\;x > 1:\;{x^{12}} > {x^9}\& {x^4} > x\]
\[ \Rightarrow \;{x^{12}} - {x^9} + {x^4} - x + 1 > 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \;\therefore \;x \in R\]

Question: |3x + 7|  <  5, then x belongs to
Solution:
\[\left| {3x + 7} \right| < 5\]
\[ \Rightarrow - 5 < 3x + 7 < 5\]
\[ \Rightarrow - 12 < 3x < - 2\]
\[ \Rightarrow x \in \left( { - 4,\frac{{ - 2}}{3}} \right)\]

Question: The integral value of x satisfing |x<sup>2</sup> + 4x + 3| + 2x + 5 = 0 is
Solution:
|x2 + 4x + 3| + 2x + 5 = 0
Observe x2 + 4x + 3 = (x + 1) (x + 3)
|x2 + 4x + 3| = \[\left\{ \begin{gathered}
  \left( {{x^2} + 4x + 3} \right),\;x < - 3\;OR\;x > - 1 \\
   - \left( {{x^2} + 4x + 3} \right),\; - 3 < x < - 1 \\
\end{gathered} \right.\]
Now if x < -3 OR x > -1
(x2 + 4x + 3) + 2x + 5 = 0
⇒ x2 + 6x + 8 = 0
⇒ (x + 4) (x + 2) = 0
x = -4, x = -2
but x < -3 OR x > -1 So, x = -4
if -3 < x < -1
- (x2 + 4x + 3) + 2x + 5 = 0
x2 + 2x - 2 = 0
\[x = \frac{{ - 2 \pm \sqrt {4 + 8} }}{2} = - 1 \pm \sqrt 3 \]
but - 3 < x < -1, So, \[x = - 1 - \sqrt 3 \]
Thus, \[x = - 4,\;( - 1 - \sqrt 3 )\]

Question: Indicate the correct alternative : The number log<sub>2</sub>7 is
Solution:
Let \[{\log _2}7 = p/q\]
(where p and q are relatively prime and q ≠ 0)
\[ \Rightarrow 7 = {2^{\frac{p}{q}}}\]
\[ \Rightarrow {7^q} = {2^p}\]
only possible for \[p = q = 0\] as LHS is odd and RHS even (which is contradiction)
\[ \Rightarrow {\log _2}7\] is irrational

Question: \[x > \sqrt {2 - {x^2}} \]
Solution:
\[x > \sqrt {2 - {x^2}} \,\,\,(Clearly\,x > 0)\]
Squaring both sides
\[{x^2} > 2 - {x^2}\]
\[ \Rightarrow {x^2} > 1\]
\[ \Rightarrow (x + 1)(x - 1) > 0\]

\[ \Rightarrow x \in (1,\infty )\,\,\,\,...(i)\]
but \[2 - {x^2} \geqslant 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow {x^2} - 2 \leqslant 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \left( {x + \sqrt 2 } \right)\left( {x - \sqrt 2 } \right) \leqslant 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow x \in \left[ {0,\sqrt 2 } \right]\,\,\,\,...(ii)\]
\[(i) \cap (ii) \Rightarrow x \in \left( {1,\sqrt 2 } \right]\]

Question: The value of x, log<sub>½</sub> x ≥ log<sub>1/3</sub> x is
Solution:
\[{\log _{\frac{1}{2}}}x \geqslant {\log _{\frac{1}{3}}}x\]
\[ \Rightarrow - {\log _2}x \geqslant - {\log _3}x\]
\[ \Rightarrow {\log _2}x \leqslant {\log _3}x\]
\[ \Rightarrow x \in \left( {0,1} \right]\] (Use Graphs)

Question: The set of values of x satisfying the inequalities (x – 1) (x – 2)  <  0  and  (3x – 7) (2x – 3)  >  0 is
Solution:
\[(x - 1)(x - 2) < 0\,and\,(3x - 7)(2x - 3) > 0\]

\[ \Rightarrow x \in \left( {1,\frac{3}{2}} \right)\]

Question: The sum of the real roots of the equation x<sup>2</sup> + |x| – 6 = 0 is
Solution:
\[\left| x \right| = \left\{ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {x\,when\,x \geqslant 0} \\
  { - x\,when\,x < 0}
\end{array}} \right.\]
Case - 1 : when \[x \geqslant 0\] then given equation becomes.
\[{x^2} + x - 6 = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \left( {x + 3} \right)\left( {x - 2} \right) = 0\]
x = 2, -3 only x = 2 is possible value because \[x \geqslant 0\]
Case - 2: when x < 0 then given equation becomes.
\[{x^2} - x - 6 = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \left( {x + 2} \right)\left( {x - 3} \right) = 0\]
x = -2, 3 only x = -2 is possible values because x < 0
so sum of roots = 2 + (-2) = 0

Question: The range of k for which $\left| {\left| {x - 1} \right| - 5} \right| = k$ have four distinct solution
Solution:
$f\left( x \right) = \left| {\left| {x - 1} \right| - 5} \right|$
Case I $x \geqslant 1$
$f\left( x \right) = \left| {x - 1 - 5} \right|$
$ = \left| {x - 6} \right| = \left\{ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {x - 6}&{x \geqslant 6} \\
  { - x + 6}&{1 \leqslant x < 6}
\end{array}} \right.$
Case II $x < 1$
$f\left( x \right) = \left| {x + 4} \right|$
\[ = \left\{ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {x + 4}&{ - 4 \leqslant x < 1} \\
  { - \left( {x + 4} \right)}&{x < - 4}
\end{array}} \right.\]

Question:
The product of the real roots of the equation
|2x + 3|2 – 3 |2x + 3| + 2 = 0, is
Solution:
\[\left| {2x + 3} \right| = \left\{ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {2x + 3\,{\text{when}}\,x \geqslant \frac{{ - 3}}{2}} \\
  { - \left( {2x + 3} \right)\,{\text{when}}\,x < \frac{{ - 3}}{2}}
\end{array}} \right.\]
Case - 1 : when \[x \geqslant \frac{{ - 3}}{2}\] then given equation becomes.
\[{\left( {2x + 3} \right)^2} - 3\left( {2x + 3} \right) + 2 = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow 2{x^2} + 3x + 1 = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \left( {x + 1} \right)\left( {2x + 1} \right) = 0\]
\[x = - 1, - \frac{1}{2}\]
Case - 2: when \[x < - \frac{3}{2}\]then given equation becomes.
\[{\left( {2x + 3} \right)^2} + 3\left( {2x + 3} \right) + 2 = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow 2{x^2} + 9x + 10 = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \left( {x + 2} \right)\left( {2x + 5} \right) = 0\]
\[x = - 2,\frac{{ - 5}}{2}\]
So product of real root is \[\left( { - 1} \right)\left( {\frac{{ - 1}}{2}} \right)\left( { - 2} \right)\left( {\frac{{ - 5}}{2}} \right) = \frac{5}{2}\].

Question: Solve for x : \[{3^{\left( {{x^2}\,-\,2} \right)}}\,\, < \,\,{\left( {\frac{1}{3}} \right)^{\left( {1\,-\,\frac{3}{2}\left| x \right|} \right)}}\]
Solution:
\[{3^{\left( {{x^2} - 2} \right)}} < {\left( {\frac{1}{3}} \right)^{\left( {1 - \frac{3}{2}\left| x \right|} \right)}}\]
\[ \Rightarrow {3^{{x^2} - 2}} < {3^{\left( {\frac{{3\left| x \right|}}{2} - 1} \right)}}\]
\[ \Rightarrow {x^2} - 2 < \frac{{3\left| x \right|}}{2} - 1\]
\[ \Rightarrow 2{x^2} - 4 < 3\left| x \right| - 2\]
\[ \Rightarrow 2{x^2} - 3\left| x \right| - 2 < 0\]
We know \[{x^2} = |x{|^2}\]
\[ \Rightarrow 2{\left| x \right|^2} - 3\left| x \right| - 2 < 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow 2{\left| x \right|^2} - 4\left| x \right| + \left| x \right| - 2 < 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow 2\left| x \right|\left( {\left| x \right| - 2} \right) + 1\left( {\left| x \right| - 2} \right) < 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \left( {2\left| x \right| + 1} \right)\left( {\left| x \right| - 2} \right) < 0\]
\[\because 2\left| x \right| + 1 > 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \left| x \right| - 2 < 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow x \in ( - 2,2)\]

Question: The equation \[\sqrt {3{x^2} + x + 5}  = x - 3,\] where x is real, has how many solutions.
Solution:
3x2 + x + 5 > 0
This is because b2 - 4ac = 1 - 4 (5) (3) = -59 [Roots are imaginary)
\[\sqrt {3{x^2} + x + 5} = x - 3\]
Squaring both sides
3x2 + x + 5 = (x-3)2
⇒ 3x2 + x + 5 = x2 + 9 - 6x
⇒2x2 + 7x - 4 = 0
⇒(2x - 1) (x + 4) = 0
⇒\[x = \frac{1}{2}\;\;OR\;x = - 4\]
but x > 3 because output of square root is positive
∴ No solution.

Question: Solution of  \[\left| {x + \frac{1}{x}} \right|\] < 4 is
Solution:
\[\left| {x + \frac{1}{x}} \right| < 4\]
\[ \Rightarrow - 4 < x + \frac{1}{x} < 4\]
\[ \Rightarrow x + \frac{1}{x} > - 4\,and\,x + \frac{1}{x}\, < 4\]
\[ \Rightarrow \frac{{{x^2} + 4x + 1}}{x} > 0\,and\,\frac{{{x^2} - 4x + 1}}{x} < 0\]

\[ \Rightarrow x \in \left( { - 2 - \sqrt 3 , - 2 + \sqrt 3 } \right) \cup \left( {2 - \sqrt 3 ,2 + \sqrt 3 } \right)\]

Question: If  –5 < x < 4, then :
Solution:
\[ - 5 < x < 4\]
\[ \Rightarrow 0 \leqslant \left| x \right| < 5\]

Question: The number of real solutions of \[\sqrt {{x^2} - 4x + 3}  = \sqrt {{x^2} - 9}  = \sqrt {4{x^2} - 14x + 6} \] is
Solution:
\[\sqrt {{x^2} - 4x + 3} = \sqrt {{x^2} - 9} = \sqrt {4{x^2} - 14x + 6} \]
(i) (ii) (iii)
From (i) and (ii)
\[{x^2} - 4x + 3 = {x^2} - 9\]
\[ \Rightarrow 4x = 12\]
\[ \Rightarrow x = 3\]
From (i) and (ii)
\[{x^2} - 9 = 4{x^2} - 14x + 6\]
\[ \Rightarrow 3{x^2} - 14x + 15 = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow 3{x^2} - 9x - 5x + 15 = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow 3x(x - 3) - 5(x - 3) = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow x = \frac{5}{3},3\]
but \[x \ne \frac{5}{3}\] (not in domain)
from (i) and (iii)
\[{x^2} - 4x + 3 = 4{x^2} - 14x + 6\]
\[ \Rightarrow 3{x^2} - 10x + 3 = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow 3{x^2} - 9x - x + 3 = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow 3x(x - 3) - 1(x - 3) = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow x = 3,\frac{1}{3}\]
\[x \ne \frac{1}{3}\](not in domain)
⇒ only one real solution x = 3.

Question: If  x<sup>2</sup> + 6x – 27 > 0 and x<sup>2</sup> – 3x – 4 < 0, then
Solution:
\[{x^2} + 6x - 27 > 0\,and\,{x^2} - 3x - 4 < 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow (x + 9)(x - 3) > 0\,and\,(x - 4)(x + 1) < 0\]

\[ \Rightarrow x \in (3,4)\]

Question: Solution of \[\frac{{x - 7}}{{x + 3}}\; > 2\] is
Solution:
\[\frac{{x - 7}}{{x + 3}} > 2\]
\[ \Rightarrow \frac{{x - 7 - 2x - 6}}{{x + 3}} > 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \frac{{ - x - 13}}{{x + 3}} > 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \frac{{x + 13}}{{x + 3}} < 0\]

\[ \Rightarrow x \in ( - 13, - 3)\]

Question: The number of real solutions of the equation ${\log _{0.5}}x = \left| x \right|$is
Solution:
Draw graph of ${\log _{0.5}}x\,and\,y = \left| x \right|$

Clearly one solution

Question: The value of x for which \[\frac{{x - 3}}{4}\,\, - \,\,x\,\, < \,\,\frac{{x - 1}}{2}\,\, - \,\,\frac{{x - 2}}{3}\] and 2 – x > 2x – 8
Solution:
\[\frac{{x - 3}}{4} - x < \frac{{x - 1}}{2} - \frac{{x - 2}}{3}\,\,and\,\,2 - x > 2x - 8\]
\[ \Rightarrow \frac{{x - 3 - 4x}}{4} < \frac{{3x - 3 - 2x + 4}}{6}\,and\,3x < 10\]
\[ \Rightarrow - 18x - 18 < 4x + 4\,and\,x < \frac{{10}}{3}\]
\[ \Rightarrow 22x > - 22\,and\,x < \frac{{10}}{3}\]
\[ \Rightarrow x > - 1\,and\,x < \frac{{10}}{3}\]
\[ \Rightarrow x \in \left( { - 1,\frac{{10}}{3}} \right)\]

Question: If  –2  <  x  <  3, then :
Solution:
\[ - 2 < x < 3\]
\[ \Rightarrow 0 \leqslant {x^2} < 9\]

Question: <!--StartFragment-->Find the sum of all positive integral values of $a$ for which every solution of the system of equations $x + ay = 3$ and $ax + 4y = 6$ which satisfies both the inequalities $x > 1$ and $y > 0$.<!--EndFragment-->
Solution:
By solving we get \[x = \frac{6}{{2 + a}}\left( {a \ne 2} \right)\]
\[y = \frac{3}{{2 + a}}\left( {a \ne 2} \right)\]
\[x > 1 \Rightarrow \frac{6}{{2 + a}} > 1 \Rightarrow \frac{{a - 4}}{{a + 2}} < 0 \Rightarrow a \in \left( { - 2,4} \right)\]
\[y > 0 \Rightarrow a \in \left( { - 2,\infty } \right)\]
Hence a $ \in \left( { - 2,4} \right) - \left\{ 2 \right\}$

Question:
Product of real roots of the equation
x2 + |x| + 9 = 0
Solution:
\[{x^2} + |x| + 9 = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \;|x{|^2} + |x| + 9 = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \;\exists \;{\text{no}}\;{\text{real}}\;{\text{roots}}\;\left( {\because \;D < 0} \right)\]

Question: Solution of |3x – 2| ≥ 1 is
Solution:
\[\left| {3x - 2} \right| \geqslant 1\]
\[ \Rightarrow 3x - 2 \geqslant 1\,or\,3x - 2 \leqslant - 1\]
\[ \Rightarrow x \geqslant 1\,or\,x \leqslant \frac{1}{3}\]
\[ \Rightarrow x \in \left( { - \infty ,\frac{1}{3}} \right] \cup \left[ {1,\infty } \right)\]

Question: Let \[f\left( x \right) - \frac{{x - \left[ x \right]}}{{1 + x - \left[ x \right]}},\,x \in R\], then the range of f is :
Solution:
For $x \in I,\,f\left( x \right) = 0$
For \[x \in R \sim I\]
\[\left( {x - \left[ x \right]} \right) < 1\]
$2 - x - \left[ x \right] < 1 + x - \left[ x \right]$
Thus $f\left( x \right) < 1/2$

Question: \[\left| {\frac{{{x^2} + 6}}{{5x}}} \right|\,\, \geqslant \,\,1\]
Solution:
\[\frac{{{x^2} + 6}}{{5x}} \geqslant 1\,or\,\frac{{{x^2} + 6}}{{5x}} \leqslant - 1\]
\[ \Rightarrow \frac{{{x^2} - 5x + 6}}{{5x}} \geqslant 0\,or\,\frac{{{x^2} + 5x + 6}}{{5x}} \leqslant 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \frac{{(x - 2)(x - 3)}}{x} \geqslant 0\,or\,\frac{{(x + 2)(x + 3)}}{x} \leqslant 0\]

\[ \Rightarrow x \in \left( {0,2} \right] \cup \left[ {3,\infty } \right)\,or\,x \in \left( { - \infty , - 3} \right] \cup \left[ { - 2,0} \right)\]

Question: The number of real solutions of the equation e<sup>x</sup> = x is
Solution: No intersection hence no solution

Question: If c < d, x<sup>2</sup> + (c + d) x + cd < 0, then x belongs to.
Solution:
given \[c < d \Rightarrow - c > - d\]
\[{x^2} + (c + d)x + cd < 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow (x + c)(x + d) < 0\]

\[ \Rightarrow x \in ( - d, - c)\]

Question:
If [x]2 = [x + 2], where [x] = the greatest integer less than or equal to x, then x must be such that

where [.] represents greatest integer function.
Solution:
\[{[x]^2} = [x] + 2\]
\[ \Rightarrow {[x]^2} - [x] - 2 = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow ([x] - 2)\left( {[x] + 1} \right) = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow [x] = - 1\,or\,[x] = 2\]
\[ \Rightarrow x \in \left[ { - 1,0} \right)\,or\,x \in \left[ {2,3} \right)\]
\[ \Rightarrow \;x \in \left[ { - 1,\;0} \right)\; \cup \;\left[ {2,3} \right)\]

Question: Find the value of a for which ${\text{m}} = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }}$is a root of equation. $a{m^2} + (\sqrt 3  - \sqrt 2 ){\text{m}} - 1 = 0$
Solution:
Put
$\begin{gathered}
  {\text{m}} = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }} \\
   \Rightarrow \frac{{\text{a}}}{3} + (\sqrt 3 - \sqrt 2 )\frac{1}{{\sqrt 3 }} - 1 = 0 \\
   \Rightarrow {\text{a}} + (3 - \sqrt 6 ) - 3 = 0 \\
   \Rightarrow a = \sqrt 6 \\
\end{gathered} $

Question: Identify the quadratic equation from the following.
Solution:
Upon simplifying
$\begin{gathered}
  {\text{m}} + \frac{1}{{\;{\text{m}}}} = 1 \\
  \frac{{{m^2} + 1}}{m} = 1 \Rightarrow {m^2} + 1 = m \Rightarrow {m^2} - m + 1 = 0 \\
  {\text{which}}\,{\text{of}}\,{\text{the}}\,{\text{form}}\,\,a{x^2} + bx + c = 0 \\
\end{gathered} $

Question: Two forces, each equal to F, act as shown in figure. Their resultant is
Solution:
$ = \sqrt {{F^2} + {F^2} + 2\;{F^2}\cos \left( {120^\circ } \right)} $
$ = \sqrt {2\;{F^2} - {F^2}} \;\;\;{\kern 1pt} \left[ {\because \cos 120^\circ = - 1} \right)$
$ = F$

Question: If $\overrightarrow A $ and $\overrightarrow B $denote the sides of a parallelogram and its area is $\frac{1}{2}{\text{AB}}$(A and B are the magnitude of $\overrightarrow A $ and $\overrightarrow B $respectively), the angle between $\overrightarrow A $ and $\overrightarrow B $is
Solution:
Type of parallelogram with $\overrightarrow A $ and $\overrightarrow B $ as adjacent side.
${\text{ Area }} = |\overrightarrow A \times \overrightarrow B | = AB\sin \theta = \frac{{AB}}{2}\;\;\;{\kern 1pt} {\text{ }}({\text{given}}){\text{ }}$
$ \Rightarrow \sin \theta = \frac{1}{2}$
$ \Rightarrow \theta = 30^\circ $

Question: A particle undergoes three successive displacements given by ${\vec S_1} = \sqrt 2 \,m$ north-east, ${\vec S_2} = 2\,m$ due south and ${\vec S_3} = 4\,m,\,30^\circ $ north of west, then magnitude of net displacement is :
Solution:
Taking this coordinate system
$\begin{gathered}
   \Rightarrow {S_1} = \sqrt 2 \left( {\frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}} \right)\widehat {\text{i}} + \sqrt 2 \left( {\frac{{ - 1}}{{\sqrt 2 }}} \right)\widehat {\text{j}} = \widehat {\text{i}} - \widehat {\text{j}} \\
  {{\text{S}}_2} = - 2\widehat {\text{i}} \\
  {{\text{S}}_3} = 4\left( {\frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2}} \right)( - \widehat {\text{i}}) + 4\left( {\frac{1}{2}} \right)\widehat {\text{j}} = 2\sqrt 3 ( - \widehat {\text{i}}) + 2\widehat {\text{j}} \\
\end{gathered} $
Net displacement $\overrightarrow {\text{S}} = {\overrightarrow {\text{S}} _1} + {\overrightarrow {\text{S}} _2} + {\overrightarrow {\text{S}} _3} = + \widehat {\text{i}}( - 1 + 2\sqrt 3 ) + \widehat {\text{j}}$
$\begin{gathered}
   = \sqrt {{{( - 1 + 2\sqrt 3 )}^2} + {1^2}} \\
   = \sqrt {14 - 4\sqrt 3 } \\
\end{gathered} $

Question: If \[\overrightarrow {\text{A}}  = 2\widehat {\text{i}} + 3\widehat {\text{j}} + 4\widehat {\text{k}}\,{\text{and}}\,\overrightarrow {\text{B}}  = 4\widehat {\text{i}} + 3\widehat {\text{j}} + 2\widehat {\text{k}},{\text{then}}\,{\text{angle}}\,{\text{between}}\,\vec A\,{\text{and}}\,\vec B\]is
Solution:
\[\begin{gathered}
  \vec A = 2\hat i + 3\hat j + 4\hat k\,{\text{and}}\,\vec B = 4\hat i + 3\hat j + 2\hat k \\
  {\text{Let}}\,{\text{angle}}\,{\text{between}}\,\overrightarrow A \,{\text{and}}\,\overrightarrow B \,{\text{is}}\,\,\theta \, \\
  \overrightarrow A \cdot \overrightarrow B = AB\cos \theta \\
   \Rightarrow (8 + 9 + 8) = (\sqrt {4 + 9 + 16} )(\sqrt {16 + 9 + 4} )\cos \theta \\
   \Rightarrow \cos \theta = \frac{{25}}{{{{(\sqrt {29} )}^2}}} \\
   \Rightarrow \theta = {\cos ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{{25}}{{{{(\sqrt {29} )}^2}}}} \right) \\
   \Rightarrow \theta = {\cos ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{{25}}{{29}}} \right) \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question: If the resultant of the vectors $(\hat i + 2\hat j - \hat k),(\hat i - \hat j + 2\hat k)\,\,{\text{and}}\,\,\overrightarrow C $ is a unit vector along the y-direction, then $\overrightarrow {\text{C}} $ is
Solution:
$\begin{gathered}
  \vec A = \hat i + 2\hat j - \hat k; \\
  \vec B = \hat i - \hat j + 2\hat k \\
  Let\;\;\;{\kern 1pt} \vec C = x\hat i + y\hat j + z\hat k \\
  \vec A + \vec B + \vec C = 1\hat j \\
   \Rightarrow (2 + x)\hat i + (1 + y)\hat j + (1 + z)\hat k = 1\hat j \\
   \to {\text{Equating}}\,{\text{components}}. \\
  2 + {\text{x}} = 0 \\
   \Rightarrow {\text{x}} = - 2 \\
  1 + {\text{y}} = 1 \Rightarrow {\text{y}} = 0 \\
  1 + {\text{z}} = 0 \Rightarrow {\text{z}} = - 1 \\
   \Rightarrow \overrightarrow {\text{C}} = - 2\widehat {\text{i}} - \widehat {\text{k}} \\
\end{gathered} $

Question: Three forces of magnitudes 6 N, 6 N and $\sqrt {72} $ N act at a corner of a cube along three sides as shown in figure. Resultant of these forces is
Solution:
Let sides OC, OG and OA represent x,y and z respectively.
\[\begin{gathered}
   \Rightarrow {\text{Resultant}}\,{\text{of}}\,{\text{forces}} = 6\hat i + \sqrt {72} \hat j + 6\hat k = \vec R \\
   \Rightarrow R = \sqrt {36 + 72 + 36} = \sqrt {144} = 12\;{\text{N}} \\
   \to {\text{Resultant}}\,{\text{of}}\,OA\,\,{\text{and}}\,\,OC\,\,{\text{is}}\,{\text{along}}\,\,OB\,\,{\text{and}}\,{\text{of}}\,{\text{magnitude}}\,\sqrt {72} .\,\,\,(\because {\text{they}}\,{\text{are}}\,{\text{perpendicular}}) \\
  \therefore {\text{Resultant}}\,{\text{of}}\,OC,OG\,\,{\text{and}}\,OA\,\,{\text{is}}\,{\text{same}}\,{\text{as}}\,\,{\text{OB}}\,(\sqrt {72} ) + \operatorname{OG} \,(\sqrt {72} ) \\
\end{gathered} \]
Its resultant will be along OE

Question: Two vectors $\overrightarrow A $ and $\overrightarrow B $are such that $\vec A + \vec B = \overrightarrow C $and A<sup>2 </sup>+ B<sup>2</sup> = C<sup>2</sup>. If θ is the angle between positive direction of $\overrightarrow A $ and $\overrightarrow B $then the correct statement is
Solution:
$\begin{gathered}
  {\kern 1pt} \overrightarrow A + \overrightarrow B = \overrightarrow C \,\,{\text{and}}\,\,{A^2} + {B^2} = {C^2}\,\,\,\,...\left( 1 \right) \\
   \Rightarrow {C^2} = {(\vec A + \vec B)^2} = {A^2} + {B^2} + 2AB\cos \theta \,\,\,\,...\left( 2 \right) \\
   \Rightarrow 2AB\cos \theta = 0 \\
   \Rightarrow \cos \theta = 0\;\;\;{\kern 1pt} ({\text{By}}\,(1){\text{ and }}(2)) \\
   \Rightarrow \theta = \frac{\pi }{2} \\
\end{gathered} $

Question: The X and Y components of vector \[\vec A\] have numerical values 6 and 6 respectively and that of \[\left( {\vec A + \vec B} \right)\] have numerical values 10 and 9. What is the magnitude of \[\vec B\]?
Solution:
$$\vec A = 6\hat i + 6\hat j$$

$$\vec A + \vec B = 10\hat i + 9\hat j$$

$$\therefore \,\vec B = \left( {10 - 6} \right)\hat i + \left( {9 - 6} \right)\hat j$$

$$ = 4\hat i + 3\hat j$$

$$\left| {\vec B} \right| = \sqrt {{4^2} + {3^2}}  = \sqrt {25}  = 5$$

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Topic: Addition of vectors using triangles law

Question: Given that $\overrightarrow A  + \overrightarrow B  + \overrightarrow C  = 0.$ Two out of the three vectors are equal in magnitude. The magnitude of the third vector is $\sqrt 2 $ times that of the other two. Which of the following can be the angles between these vectors?
Solution:
$\overrightarrow A + \overrightarrow B + \overrightarrow C = 0.$
Let $A = B$ and $C = \sqrt 2 \;A = \sqrt 2 \;B$
$\begin{gathered}
  \overrightarrow C = - (\overrightarrow A + \overrightarrow B ) \\
  {C^2} = {A^2} + {B^2} + 2AB\cos \theta \\
\end{gathered} $
$ \Rightarrow 2\;{{\text{A}}^2} = 2\;{{\text{A}}^2} + 2\;{{\text{A}}^2}\cos \theta $
$ \Rightarrow \theta = 90^\circ $
$ \Rightarrow $ Angle between $\vec A$ and $\vec B$ is $90^\circ $
$ \Rightarrow $ Angle of $\left( {\vec A + \vec B} \right)$ with $\vec A$ or $\vec B$ is $45^\circ $
$ \Rightarrow \vec c$ is opposite to $\left( {\vec A + \vec B} \right)$, i.e., at $180^\circ $ to $\vec A + \vec B$
${\text{and}}\,{\text{of}}\,135^\circ \,{\text{with}}\,\,\overrightarrow A \,\,{\text{or}}\,\,\overrightarrow B $

Question: The simple sum of two co-initial vectors is 16 units. Their vectors sum is 8 units. The resultant of the vectors is perpendicular to the smaller vector. The magnitudes of the two vectors are :
Solution:
Co -initial vectors are those, which start with same point.
Given A + B =16 ...(i)
$\overrightarrow {\text{A}} + \overrightarrow {\text{B}} = 8$
Since, resultant is perpendicular to smaller vector
$\begin{gathered}
  \tan \alpha = \frac{{{\text{B}}\sin \theta }}{{{\text{A}} + {\text{B}}\cos \theta }} = \infty \\
   \Rightarrow {\text{A}} + {\text{B}}\cos \theta = 0 \\
   \Rightarrow \cos \theta = \frac{{ - {\text{A}}}}{{\text{B}}} \\
\end{gathered} $
$\begin{gathered}
  {\text{Now}},{(\overrightarrow {\text{A}} + \overrightarrow {\text{B}} )^2} = {{\text{A}}^2} + {{\text{B}}^2} + 2{\text{AB}}\cos \theta \\
   \Rightarrow 64 = {{\text{A}}^2} + {{\text{B}}^2} + 2{\text{AB}}\left( { - \frac{{\text{A}}}{{\text{B}}}} \right) = {{\text{B}}^2} - {{\text{A}}^2} \\
   \Rightarrow 64 = ({\text{A}} + {\text{B}})({\text{B}} - {\text{A}}) \\
   \Rightarrow {\text{B}} - {\text{A}} = 4\;\;\;{\kern 1pt} \ldots {\text{ (ii) }} \\
   \Rightarrow {\text{By}}\,({\text{i}}){\text{ and (ii) }} \\
  {\text{B}} = 10;{\text{A}} = 6 \\
\end{gathered} $

Question: The magnitude of the X and Y components of $\overrightarrow A $ are 7 and 6. Also the magnitudes of X and Y components of $\overrightarrow A  + \overrightarrow B $are 11 and 9 respectively. What is the magnitude of $\overrightarrow B $ ?
Solution:
$\overrightarrow {\text{A}} = 7\widehat {\text{i}} + 6\widehat {\text{j}}$
\[\begin{gathered}
  \overrightarrow {\text{B}} = x\widehat {\text{i}} + y\widehat {\text{j}} + {\text{z}}\widehat {\text{k}} \\
  \overrightarrow {\text{A}} + \overrightarrow {\text{B}} = 11\widehat {\text{i}} + 9\widehat {\text{j}} \\
   \Rightarrow (7 + {\text{x}})\widehat {\text{i}} + (6 + {\text{y}})\widehat {\text{j}} + {\text{z}}\widehat {\text{k}} = 11\widehat {\text{i}} + 9\widehat {\text{j}} \\
   \to {\text{ Equanting components}}{\text{. }} \\
  7 + {\text{x}} = 11 \\
   \Rightarrow {\text{x}} = 4 \\
\end{gathered} \]
$\begin{gathered}
  6 + {\text{y}} = 9 \\
   \Rightarrow {\text{y}} = 3{\text{z}} = 0 \\
   \Rightarrow {\text{z}} = 0 \\
   \Rightarrow \overrightarrow {\text{B}} = 4\widehat {\text{i}} + 3\widehat {\text{j}} \\
  \therefore {\text{B}} = \sqrt {{4^2} + {3^2}} = 5{\text{ units}}{\text{. }} \\
\end{gathered} $

Question: If a vector $\overrightarrow P $ making angles α , β , and \[\gamma \] respectively with the X, Y and Z axes respectively, then ${\sin ^2}\alpha + {\sin ^2}\beta + {\sin ^2}\gamma = $
Solution:
${\sin ^2}\alpha + {\sin ^2}\beta + \sin \gamma $ $ = 1 - {\cos ^2}\alpha + 1 - {\cos ^2}\beta + 1 - {\cos ^2}\gamma $
$ = 3 - ({\cos ^2}\alpha + {\cos ^2}\beta + {\cos ^2}\gamma )$$ = 3 - 1 = 2$

$\begin{gathered}
  (\overrightarrow {\text{B}} \times \overrightarrow {\text{A}} ) \bot \overrightarrow {\text{A}} \\
  {\text{dot}}\,\,{\text{product}}\,\,{\text{of}}\,\,{\text{perpendicuar}}\,\,{\text{vector}}\,\,{\text{is}}\,\,{\text{zero}} \\
   \Rightarrow \overrightarrow {\text{A}} \cdot (\overrightarrow {\text{B}} \times \overrightarrow {\text{A}} ) = 0 \\
\end{gathered} $

Question: A vector $\vec A$ points vertically upwards and $\vec B$ point towards North. The vector product $\overrightarrow {\text{A}}  \times \overrightarrow {\text{B}} $ is
Solution: By right hand thumb rule, if we point our fingers towards up $(\vec A)$ and curl them towards north $(\vec B),$then our thumb point towards west $(\overrightarrow {\text{A}} \times \overrightarrow {\text{B}} ).$

Question: The sides of two square plots are (2x − 1)m and (5x + 4 )m . The area of the second square plot is 9 times the area of the first square plot. Find the side of the larger plot.
Solution:
$\begin{gathered}
  {(5x + 4)^2} = 9{(2x - 1)^2} \\
   \Rightarrow 5x + 4 = \pm 3(2x - 1) \\
   \Rightarrow 5x + 4 + 6x - 3 = 0{\text{ Or }}5x + 4 - 6x + 3 = 0 \\
   \Rightarrow 11x = - 1{\text{ Or }}5x + 4 - 6x + 3 = 0 \\
   \Rightarrow - x + 7 = 0 \\
  \, \Rightarrow x = 7 \\
\end{gathered} $
So the larger side will be given by 5x + 4 which will be 39 at
x = 7.

Question: Evaluate $\sqrt {65} $ correct up to four decimal places.
Solution:
$\begin{gathered}
  \sqrt {65} = {(64 + 1)^{\frac{1}{2}}} \\
   \Rightarrow \sqrt {65} = 8{\left( {1 + \frac{1}{{64}}} \right)^{\frac{1}{2}}} \\
\end{gathered} $
As value$\frac{1}{{64}}{\text{ }}$is very small, we can neglect its higher powers
$\begin{gathered}
  {\text{So, }}{(1 + {\text{x}})^{\text{n}}} = 1 + {\text{nx}} \\
   \Rightarrow \sqrt {65} = 8\left( {1 + \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{{64}}} \right) \\
   \Rightarrow \sqrt {65} = 8 + \frac{1}{{16}} = 8.0625 \\
\end{gathered} $

Question: A body moves 6 m north, 8 m east and 10m vertically upwards, what is its resultant displacement from initial position (only magnitude)
Solution:
\[\overrightarrow S = 6\widehat i + 8\widehat j + 10\hat k\]

\[{\text{S}} = {\left( {{6^2} + {8^2} + {{10}^2}} \right)^{1/2}} = \sqrt {200} = (10\sqrt 2 \;\,{\text{m}})\]

Question: The area of the parallelogram represented by the vectors, $\vec A = 4\hat i + 3\hat j\,\,{\text{and}}\,\,\vec B = 2\hat i + 4\hat j$as adjacent side is
Solution:
\[\begin{gathered}
  \vec A = 4\hat i + 3\hat j\,\,{\text{and}}\,\,\vec B = 2\hat i + 4\hat j \\
  {\text{Area}}\,{\text{of}}\,{\text{paralleogram}}\,{\text{with}}\,\overrightarrow A \,\,{\text{and}}\,\,\overrightarrow B \,{\text{as}}\,{\text{adjacentside}}{\text{. Area}} = \,|\overrightarrow A \times \overrightarrow B |\, = \,\left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\widehat i}&{\widehat j}&{\widehat k} \\
  4&3&0 \\
  2&4&0
\end{array}} \right| \\
   = |\widehat i(0) - \widehat j(0) + \widehat k(16 - 6)| = 10{\text{ units}}.{\text{ }} \\

Question: Given $\vec A = \hat i + 2\hat j - 3\hat k.$ When a vector $\vec B$ is added to $\overrightarrow A $ , we get a unit vector along X–axis. Then, $\vec B$is
Solution:
$\overrightarrow {\text{A}} = \widehat {\text{i}} + 2\widehat {\text{j}} - 3\widehat {\text{k}}$
\[\begin{gathered}
  {\text{Let}}\,\vec B = x\hat i + y\hat j + z\hat k\, \\
  {\text{Given}}\,\overrightarrow A + \overrightarrow B = \widehat i \\
   \Rightarrow (1 + x)\hat i + (2 + y)\hat j + (z - 3)\hat k = 1\hat i \\
  {\text{Since}},x,y\,\,{\text{and}}\,\,z\,{\text{components}}\,{\text{are}}\,{\text{independent}}\,{\text{to}}\,{\text{each}}\,{\text{other}}. \\
  {\text{Equating}}\,{\text{them}}\,{\text{on}}\,{\text{eighter}}\,{\text{side}}.1 + x = 1 \\
   \Rightarrow x = 0 \\
  2 + y = 0 \\
   \Rightarrow y = - 2z - 3 = 0 \\
   \Rightarrow z = 3 \\
   \Rightarrow \vec B = - 2\hat j + 3\hat k \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question: Two vectors $\overrightarrow a \,\,{\text{and}}\,\,\overrightarrow b $ are at an angle of 60° with each other. Their resultant makes an angle of 45° with $\overrightarrow a $. If $\left| {\overrightarrow b } \right|$= 2 unit, then $\left| {\overrightarrow a } \right|$is
Solution:
θ = 60°, a = ?, b = 2, α = 45°
$\begin{gathered}
  tan\alpha = \frac{{b\sin \theta }}{{a + b\cos \theta }} \\
   \Rightarrow b\sin \theta = a + b\cos \theta \\
   \Rightarrow 2\left\{ {\frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2}} \right\} = a + 2\left( {\frac{1}{2}} \right) \\
   \Rightarrow a = \sqrt 3 - 1 \\
\end{gathered} $

Question: A metal rod AB of length 10x has its one end A in ice at 0oC and the other end B in water at 100oC. If a point P on the rod is maintained at 400oC, then it is found that equal amounts of water evaporate and ice melt per unit time. The latent heat of evaporation of water is 540 cal/g and latent heat of melting of ice is 80 cal/g. If the point P is at a distance of $\lambda x$ from the end A, find the value of $\lambda $. [Neglect heat loss to the surroundings.]
Solution:
Mice melted= Msteam formed

$Q = \frac{{KAt\Delta \theta }}{l} = mL$
$m\alpha \frac{{\Delta \theta }}{{l.L}}$
$\frac{{400}}{{\lambda x \times 80}} = \frac{{300}}{{\left( {10 - \lambda } \right)x \times 540}}$
$90x - 9\lambda x = \lambda x,$$90x = 10\lambda x$
$\therefore \lambda = 9$
