Question: A vector ${\vec F_1}$ is along the positive ${\text{Y}} - $axis. If its vector product with another vector ${\overrightarrow {\text{F}} _2}$ is zero, then ${\overrightarrow {\text{F}} _2}$ could be
Solution:
\[\begin{gathered}
  {\text{Let}}\,{\overrightarrow F _1} = k\widehat j \\
  {\text{Let}}\,{{\vec F}_2} = x\hat i + y\hat j + z\hat k\,\, \\
  {\overrightarrow F _1} \times {\overrightarrow F _2} = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\widehat i}&{\widehat j}&{\widehat k} \\
  0&k&0 \\
  x&y&z
\end{array}} \right| = \widehat i(kz) - \widehat j(0) + \widehat k( - kx) = 0 \\
   \Rightarrow z = 0\,\,{\text{and}}\,\,x = 0 \\
   \Rightarrow {{\vec F}_2}\,{\text{is}}\,\,{\text{along}}\,y\,{\text{axis}} \\
   \Rightarrow {\overrightarrow F _2}\,\,{\text{can}}\,\,{\text{be}}\,\,4\widehat j \\
\end{gathered} \]

$ = \hat i\left( {8 + 9} \right) - \hat j\left( {12 - 6} \right) + \hat k\left( { - 9 - 4} \right)$

Question: If \[\left| {\vec a \times \vec b} \right| = \vec a\;.\;\vec b\] then angle between a and b is
Solution:
Since \[\left| {\overrightarrow a \times \overrightarrow b } \right|\, = \,\overrightarrow a \,.\,\overrightarrow b \,\]
\[ \Rightarrow \sin \theta = \cos \theta \]
\[ \Rightarrow \tan \theta = 1 \Rightarrow \theta = 45^\circ \]

Question: If the vectors $\vec A = 2\hat i + 4\hat j$and $\vec B = 5\hat i - \hat p$are parallel to each other, the magnitude of $\vec B$is
Solution:
$\begin{gathered}
  \vec A = 2\hat i + 4\hat j\,\,{\text{and}}\,\vec B = 5\hat i - p\,\hat j \\
  {\text{Since}},\vec A\,\,{\text{is}}\,{\text{parallel}}\,{\text{to}}\,\vec B \\
   \Rightarrow {\text{angle}}\,{\text{between}}\,{\text{the}}\,m\,\,{\text{is '}}\,0' \\
   \Rightarrow \overrightarrow A \times \overrightarrow B = AB\sin (0) = 0 \\
   \Rightarrow \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\hat i}&{\hat j}&{\hat k} \\
  2&4&0 \\
  5&{ - p}&0
\end{array}} \right| = \hat i(0) - \hat j(0) + \hat k( - 2p - 20) = 0 \\
   \Rightarrow - 2p = 20 \\
   \Rightarrow p = - 10 \\
  \vec B = 5\widehat i + 10\widehat j \\
  |\overrightarrow B | = \sqrt {25 + 100} = \sqrt {125} = 5\sqrt 5 \\
\end{gathered} $

Question: One of the rectangular components of a velocity of 60 kmh<sup>–1</sup> is 30 km h<sup>–1</sup>. The other rectangular component is
Solution:
Choose one of the directions as x and other as y.
$\begin{gathered}
  \vec v = {v_x}\hat i - {v_y}\hat j;v = 60,{v_x} = 30 \\
  {{\text{v}}^2} = {\text{v}}_{\text{x}}^2 + {\text{v}}_{\text{y}}^2 \\
   \Rightarrow {(60)^2} = {(30)^2} + {\text{v}}_{\text{y}}^2 \\
   \Rightarrow {{\text{v}}_{\text{y}}} = 30\sqrt 3 \;{\text{km}}\;{{\text{h}}^{ - 1}} \\
\end{gathered} $

Question: If vectors (x – 2) \[\hat i + \hat j\] and (x + 1) \[\hat i + 2\hat j\] are collinear, then the value of x is

An aeroplane moves 400 m towards the north, 300 m towards west and then 1200 m vertically upwards, then the difference between total distance and magnitude of resultant displacement from initial position:
Solution:
Displacement ($\vec S$):

\[\overrightarrow S = 400\widehat i + 300\widehat j + 1200\hat k\]

\[{\text{S}} = {\left[ {{{(400)}^2} + {{(300)}^2} + {{(1200)}^2}} \right]^{1/2}}\]

\[ = \sqrt {1690000} = 1300\;{\text{m}}\]

Distance (x) = 400+300+1200 =1900m

Difference = 1900-1300 = 600m

Question: The resultant of two forces, each P, acting at an angle θ is
Solution:
Resultant, ${\text{R}} = \sqrt {{{\text{P}}^2} + {{\text{P}}^2} + 2{{\text{P}}^2}\cos \theta } $
$\begin{gathered}
   = P(\sqrt {2(1 + \cos \theta )} ) \\
   = P\left( {\sqrt {2\left( {2{{\cos }^2}\theta /2} \right)} } \right)\left\{ {\because \cos 2\theta = 2{{\cos }^2}\theta - 1} \right\} \\
   = 2P\cos \frac{\theta }{2} \\
\end{gathered} $

Question: If the magnitude of the sum of the two vectors is equal to the magnitudes of their difference, then the angle between vectors is
Solution:
Given$|\vec A + \vec B|\, = \,|A - B|$
 $ \Rightarrow {A^2} + {B^2} + 2AB\cos \theta = {A^2} + {B^2} - 2AB\cos \theta $
$ \Rightarrow 4AB\cos \theta = 0$
$ \Rightarrow \cos \theta = 0$
$ \Rightarrow \theta = 90^\circ $

Question: Two vectors $\overrightarrow A $ and $\overrightarrow B $are inclined to each other at an angle θ. Which of the following is the unit vector perpendicular to both $\overrightarrow A $ and $\overrightarrow B $?
Solution:
A vector perpendicular to both $\vec A\,\,{\text{and}}\,\vec B\,\,{\text{is}}\,\,\vec C = \vec A \times \vec B$ Unit vector perpendicular to $\vec A \times \vec B$
$\begin{gathered}
  \widehat {\text{C}} = \frac{{\overrightarrow {\text{C}} }}{{|\overrightarrow {\text{C}} |}} = \frac{{\overrightarrow {\text{A}} \times \overrightarrow {\text{B}} }}{{|\overrightarrow {\text{A}} \times \overrightarrow {\text{B}} |}} = \frac{{\overrightarrow {\text{A}} \times \overrightarrow {\text{B}} }}{{{\text{AB}}\sin \theta }}{\text{ }} \\
  {\text{Also, }}\widehat {\text{C}} = \frac{{{\text{A}}(\widehat {\text{A}}) \times {\text{B}}(\widehat {\text{B}})}}{{{\text{AB}}\sin \theta }} = \frac{{\widehat {\text{A}} \times \widehat {\text{B}}}}{{\sin \theta }} \\
\end{gathered} $

Question: Given, \[\overrightarrow {\text{C}}  = \overrightarrow {\text{A}}  \times \overrightarrow {\text{B}} \,\,{\text{and}}\,\,\overrightarrow {\text{D}}  = \overrightarrow {\text{B}}  \times \overrightarrow {\text{A}} \]. What is the angle between $\vec C\,and\,\vec D?$
Solution:
\[\begin{gathered}
  \overrightarrow {\text{C}} = \overrightarrow {\text{A}} \times \overrightarrow {\text{B}} \,\,{\text{and}}\,\,\overrightarrow {\text{D}} = \overrightarrow {\text{B}} \times \overrightarrow {\text{A}} \\
   \Rightarrow \vec D = - (\vec A \times \vec B)\;\;\;{\kern 1pt} \{ \because \vec A \times \vec B = - (\vec B \times \vec A)\} \Rightarrow \overrightarrow C \,\,{\text{and}}\,\,\overrightarrow D \,\,{\text{are}}\,\,{\text{anti - parllel}}. \\
   \Rightarrow {\text{angle}}\,\,{\text{between}}\,\,\overrightarrow C \,\,{\text{and}}\,\,\overrightarrow D \,\,{\text{is}}\,\,180^\circ \\
\end{gathered} \]

Question: Given θ is the angle between $\vec A\,\,{\text{and}}\,\vec B.$ Then $|\hat A \times \hat B|$is equal to
Solution:
$\begin{gathered}
  \overrightarrow {\text{A}} \times \overrightarrow {\text{B}} = {\text{AB}}\sin \theta \\
  |\overrightarrow {\text{A}} \times \overrightarrow {\text{B}} | = {\text{AB}}\sin \theta \\
  |\widehat {\text{A}} \times \widehat {\text{B}}| = (1)(1)\sin \theta = \sin \theta \;\;\;{\kern 1pt} (\because |\widehat {\text{A}}| = |\widehat {\text{B}}| = 1) \\
\end{gathered} $

Question: Which one of the following is not the vector quantity ?
Solution: Speed has no direction, it’s not a vector.

Question: The dot product of two vectors \[ - 2\,\widehat i + 3\,\widehat j\, + \,\widehat k\,\,{\text{and}}\,\,2\,\widehat i + 2\,\widehat j\, - 4\,\widehat k\] is – n, then what is the value of n
Solution:
Let \[\overrightarrow A = - 2\,\widehat i + 3\,\widehat j\, + \,\widehat k,\,\,\overrightarrow B = 2\,\widehat i + 2\,\widehat j\, - 4\,\widehat k\,\]

\[\overrightarrow A \cdot \overrightarrow B = - 2 \times 2 + 3 \times 2 + 1 \times \left( { - 4} \right) = - 2\]

Question: The unit vector perpendicular to vectors \[\hat i + \hat j\;\,{\text{and}}\;\hat j + \hat k\]  is
Solution:
\[\vec a \times \vec b = \hat i + \hat k - \widehat j\] (Since \[\overrightarrow a \times \overrightarrow b \] is the vector \[ \bot \] to both \[\overrightarrow a \,\,\& \,\,\overrightarrow b \])
so, unit vector \[ = \pm \frac{{(\hat i - \hat j + \hat k)}}{{\sqrt 3 }}\]

Question: Given $\overrightarrow R  = \overrightarrow A  + \overrightarrow B $ and R = A = B. The angle between and $\overrightarrow B $is
Solution:
$\begin{gathered}
  \vec R = \vec A + \vec B \\
   \Rightarrow {R^2} = {A^2} + {B^2} + 2AB\cos \theta \\
\end{gathered} $
But,
A = B = R
$ \Rightarrow {A^2} = 2{A^2}(1 + \cos \theta )$
$ \Rightarrow \cos \theta = \frac{{ - 1}}{2}$
$ \Rightarrow \theta = 120^\circ $

Question: A bird moves in such a way that it has a displacement of 10 m towards east, 10 m towards north and 5 m vertically upwards. The magnitude of its displacement (in m) is
Solution:
\[\begin{gathered}
  S = \sqrt {{\ell ^2} + {b^2} + {h^2}} \\
   = \sqrt {{{(10)}^2} + {{(10)}^2} + {5^2}} \\
   = \sqrt {100 + 100 + 25} \\
   = \sqrt {225} = 15\;{\text{m}} \\

Question: For what value of a, $\vec A = 2\hat i + a\hat j + \hat k$will be perpendicular to $\vec B = 4\hat i - 2\hat j - \hat k$
Solution:
$\vec A = 2\hat i + a\hat j + \hat k\,\,{\text{and}}\,\,\vec B = 4\hat i - 2\hat j - \hat k$
For perpendicular vectors, $\theta = 90^\circ $
$ \Rightarrow \cos \theta = 0$
$ \Rightarrow \overrightarrow A \cdot \overrightarrow B = 0$
$ \Rightarrow 8 - 2a - 1 = 0$
$ \Rightarrow 2a = 7$
$ \Rightarrow a = \frac{7}{2} = 3.5$

Question: Given $\vec P = 3\hat j + 4\hat k\,\,{\text{and}}\,\,\vec Q = 2\hat j + 5\hat k.$ The scalar product of these vector is
Solution:
$\begin{gathered}
  \vec P = 3\hat j + 4\hat k\,\,{\text{and}}\,\,\vec Q = 2\hat j + 5\hat k \\
  \vec P \cdot \vec Q = \left( {{P_x}\hat i + {P_y}\hat j + {P_2}\hat k} \right) \cdot \left( {{Q_x}\hat i + {Q_y}\hat j + {Q_z}\hat k} \right) \\
   = {P_x}{Q_x} + {P_y}{Q_y} + {P_z}{Q_z} \\
   = 6 + 20 \\
   = 26 \\
\end{gathered} $

Question: The vector sum of two forces is perpendicular to their vector differences. In that case, the forces
Options: are not equal to each other in magnitude
are equal to each other in magnitude
Solution:
$\begin{gathered}
  \vec R = \vec A + \vec B\,and\,\vec P = \vec A - \vec B \\
  {\text{Given}}\,{\text{that}}\,\vec R\,\,{\text{is}}\,{\text{perpendicular}}\,{\text{to}}\,\vec P \\
   \Rightarrow \overrightarrow {\text{R}} \cdot \overrightarrow {\text{P}} = 0 \\
   \Rightarrow (\overrightarrow {\text{A}} + \overrightarrow {\text{B}} ) \cdot (\overrightarrow {\text{A}} - \overrightarrow {\text{B}} ) = 0 \\
   \Rightarrow {{\text{A}}^2} - {{\text{B}}^2} = 0 \\
   \Rightarrow {{\text{A}}^2} = {{\text{B}}^2} \\
   \Rightarrow |\overrightarrow {\text{A}} | = |\overrightarrow {\text{B}} | \\
\end{gathered} $

Question: Three forces acting on a body are shown in fig. To have the resultant force only along the y-direction, the magnitude of the minimum additional force needed along OX is
Solution:
To have the resultant force along the y-direction, net force along x-direction should be zero.


So, resultant of these 3 forces along x-direction is 0 when we apply \[{F_x}\] towards right.


\[{F_x} + \frac{1}{2} = 1\]

\[ \Rightarrow {F_x} = \frac{1}{2}N\]

Question: If vectors \[3\hat i + 2\hat j + 8\hat k\;\,{\text{and}}\,\;2\hat i + x\hat j + \hat k\] are perpendicular then -x is equal to
Solution:
\[\left( {3\hat i + 2\hat j + 8\hat k} \right).\left( {2\hat i + x\hat j + \hat k} \right) = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \;6 + 2x + 8 = 0\]
\[ \Rightarrow \;x = - 7\]

Question: If $\vec A = 2\hat i + 3\hat j + 6\hat k\,{\text{and}}\,\vec B = 3\hat i - 6\hat j + 2\hat k$then vector perpendicular to both $\overrightarrow {\text{A}} $and $\overrightarrow {\text{B}} $has magnitude ${\text{k}}\,\,{\text{times}}\,\,{\text{that}}\,\,{\text{of}}\,(6\hat i + 2\hat j - 3\hat k).$That k is equal to
Solution:
$\begin{gathered}
  \vec A = 2\hat i + 3\hat j + 6\hat k\,{\text{and}}\,\vec B = 3\hat i - 6\hat j + 2\hat k \\
  {\text{A}}\,{\text{vector}}\,{\text{perpendicular}}\,{\text{to}}\,{\text{both}}\,\vec B\,{\text{and}}\,\vec A\,\,{\text{is}}\,\,\vec A \times \vec B \\
   \Rightarrow |\overrightarrow A \times \overrightarrow B | = \left| {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}}
  {\widehat i}&{\widehat j}&{\widehat k} \\
  2&3&6 \\
  3&{ - 6}&2
\end{array}} \right| = \widehat i(6 + 36) - \widehat j(4 - 18) + \widehat k( - 12 - 9) \\
   = 42\widehat i + 14\widehat j - 21\widehat k = k(6\widehat i + 2\widehat j - 3\widehat k) \\
   \Rightarrow k = 7 \\
\end{gathered} $

Question: A force is inclined at 60° to the horizontal. If its rectangular component in the horizontal direction is 50 N, then magnitude of the force in the vertical direction is
Solution:
$\vec F = {F_x}\hat i + {F_y}\hat j$
$\tan 60^\circ = \frac{{{F_y}}}{{{F_x}}}$
$ \Rightarrow {F_y} = {F_x}\tan 60^\circ $
$ = 50\sqrt 3 $
$ = 87\;N.$

Question: The magnitude of resultant of three vectors of magnitude 1, 2 and 3 whose directions are those of the sides of an equilateral triangle taken in order is :
Solution:
$\begin{gathered}
   \Rightarrow \overrightarrow {\text{a}} = \widehat {\text{i}} \\
  \overrightarrow {\text{b}} = \left( { - \frac{1}{2}\widehat {\text{i}} + \frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2}\widehat {\text{j}}} \right)2 = - \widehat {\text{i}} + \sqrt 3 \widehat {\text{j}} \\
  \overrightarrow {\text{c}} = \left( { - \frac{1}{2}\widehat {\text{i}} - \frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2}\widehat {\text{j}}} \right)3 = \frac{{ - 3\widehat {\text{i}}}}{2} - \frac{{3\sqrt 3 }}{2}\widehat {\text{j}} \\
  \vec a + \vec b + \vec c = \frac{{ - 3\hat i}}{2} + \left( {\frac{{ - \sqrt 3 }}{2}\hat j} \right) \\
   \Rightarrow |\overrightarrow {\text{a}} + \overrightarrow {\text{b}} + \overrightarrow {\text{c}} | = \sqrt {\frac{9}{4} + \frac{3}{4}} = \sqrt {\frac{{12}}{4}} = \sqrt 3 \\
\end{gathered} $

Question: The component of vector $\vec A = {a_x}\hat i + {a_y}\hat j + {a_z}\hat k$ along the direction of $(\widehat {\text{i}} - \widehat {\text{j}})$is
Solution:
$\begin{gathered}
  \overrightarrow A = {a_x}\widehat i + {a_y}\widehat j + {a_z}\widehat k,\overrightarrow B = \widehat i - \widehat j \\
  {\text{Component}}\,{\text{of}}\,\overrightarrow A \,\,{\text{along}}\,\,\overrightarrow B = A\cos \theta = \overrightarrow A \cdot (\widehat B) \\
   = \left( {{a_x}\hat i + {a_y}\hat j + {a_z}\hat k} \right) \cdot \frac{{(\hat i - j)}}{{\sqrt 2 }} = \frac{{\left( {{a_x} - {a_y}} \right)}}{{\sqrt 2 }} \\
\end{gathered} $

Question: The number of six digit numbers that can be formed from the digits 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6 & 7 so that digits do not repeat and the terminal digits are even is :
Solution:
(Since terminal digits are fixed i.e, even. so 5 numbers at 10th place can be permuted in 5 ways)
Required Numbers

\[ = 6 \times 120 = 720\]

Question: The x and y components of a force are 2 N and –3N. The force is
Solution:
x component, ${{\text{F}}_{\text{x}}} = 2\widehat {\text{i}}$
y component, ${{\text{F}}_{\text{y}}} = - 3\widehat {\text{j}}$
$\begin{gathered}
   \Rightarrow \vec F = {F_x}\hat i + {F_y}\hat j \\
   \Rightarrow \overrightarrow {\text{F}} = 2\widehat {\text{i}} - 3\widehat {\text{j}}{\text{N}} \\
\end{gathered} $

Question: If S = 1 + a + a<sup>2</sup> +........ to  then the value of a is
Solution:
\[S = \frac{1}{{1 - a}}\]
\[ \Rightarrow 1 - a = \frac{1}{S} \Rightarrow a = \frac{{S - 1}}{S}\]

Question: If vectors $\overrightarrow A $ and $\overrightarrow B $ have an angle θ between them, then value of \[\left| {\widehat A - \widehat B} \right|\]will be :
Solution:
If angle between $\vec A\,\,{\text{and}}\,\,\vec B\,\,{\text{is}}\,\,\theta $, then between \[\vec A\,\,{\text{and}} - \vec B\,\,{\text{is}}\,\,180^\circ - \theta \]
$|\hat A - \hat B| = \sqrt {{A^2} + {B^2} + 2\hat A\hat B\cos \left( {180^\circ - \theta } \right)} $
$ = \sqrt {1 + 1 + 2\left( { - \cos \theta } \right)} $
$ = \sqrt {2(1 - \cos \theta )} $
$ = \sqrt {2\left( {2{{\sin }^2}\frac{\theta }{2}} \right)} \left( {\because \cos 2\theta = 1 - 2{{\sin }^2}\theta } \right)$
$ = 2\sin \frac{\theta }{2}$

Question: A boy stops after travelling 3 km towards east and then goes 4 km towards north along a plane road. The resultant displacement of the boy is (only magnitude)
Solution:
By Pythagoras theorem,


\[{\text{R}} = \sqrt {{3^2} + {4^2}} = 5\,{\text{km}}\]

Question: A vector is not changed if
Options: it is rotated through an arbitrary angle
it is multiplied by an arbitrary scalar
it is cross multiplied by a unit vector
it is slid parallel to itself.
Solution: A vector does not change, if its magnitude and direction are same. Thus it does not change, if it is slid parallel to itself.

Solution:
The magnitude of unit vector is 1.
$ \Rightarrow a_{\text{x}}^2 + a_{\text{y}}^2 + a_{\text{z}}^2 = 1$
$\begin{gathered}
   \Rightarrow 0.25 + 0.64 + {{\text{c}}^2} = 1 \\
   \Rightarrow {\text{c}} = \sqrt {0.11} \\
\end{gathered} $

Question: The value of (0.99)15 is
Solution:
$\begin{gathered}
  {\kern 1pt} \therefore \,\,\, = {(0.99)^{15}} = {(1.001)^{15}} \\
   = 1{ - ^{16}}{{\text{C}}_1}(0.01){ + ^{15}}{{\text{C}}_2}{(0.02)^2}{ - ^{15}}{{\text{C}}_3}{(0.01)^2} + \ldots . \\
\end{gathered} $
We want to answer correct upto 4 decimal places and as such,
we have left further expansion.
$\begin{gathered}
   = 1 - 15(0.01) + \frac{{15.14}}{{1.2}}(0.0001) - \frac{{15.14.13}}{{1.2.3}}(0.000001) + \ldots = 1 - 0.15 + 0.0105 - 0.000455 + \ldots \\
   = 1.0105 - 0.150455 \\
   = 0.8601 \\

$\begin{gathered}

Question: Projection of $\overrightarrow {\text{P}} $on $\overrightarrow {\text{Q}} $ is
Solution:
Projection of $\overrightarrow {\text{P}} $on $\overrightarrow {\text{Q}} $$ = {\text{PQ}}\cos \theta ${θ = angle between $\overrightarrow {\text{P}} $and $\overrightarrow {\text{Q}} $}
$\begin{gathered}
  \because \overrightarrow {\text{P}} \cdot \overrightarrow {\text{Q}} = {\text{PQ}}\cos \theta \\
  P\cos \theta = \frac{{\overrightarrow {\text{P}} \cdot \overrightarrow {\text{Q}} }}{{|\overrightarrow {\text{Q}} |}} = \overrightarrow {\text{P}} \cdot \widehat {\text{Q}} \\
\end{gathered} $

Question: What is the unit vector along $\hat i + \hat j?$
Solution:
Unit vector,
$\widehat {\text{a}} = \frac{{\text{a}}}{{|\overrightarrow {\text{a}} |}}$
$\begin{gathered}
  {\text{Here, }}\overrightarrow {\text{a}} = \widehat {\text{i}} + \widehat {\text{j}} \\
  \therefore \,|\overrightarrow {\text{a}} | = \sqrt {1 + 1} = \sqrt 2 \\
   \Rightarrow \overrightarrow {\text{a}} = \frac{{\widehat {\text{i}} + \widehat {\text{j}}}}{{\sqrt 2 }} \\
\end{gathered} $

Question: If n ∈ N and (1 – x + x<sup>2</sup>)<sup>n</sup> = a<sub>0</sub> + a<sub>1</sub>x + a<sub>2</sub>x<sup>2</sup> + ...... + a<sub>2n</sub>x<sup>2n</sup>, then a<sub>0</sub>+ a<sub>2</sub>+ a<sub>4</sub> + ... +a<sub>2n</sub> is equal to :
Solution:
⇒ (1 – x + x2)n = a0 + a1x + ... + a2n x2n
Put x = 1 :
⇒ 1 = a0 + a1 + ... + a2n
Put x = – 1 :
⇒ 3n = a0 – a1 + ... + a2n
Adding these
3n + 1 = 2 (a0 + a2 + ... + a2n)
a0 + a2 + ... + a2n =\[\frac{{{3^n} + 1}}{2}\]

\[{t_{r + 1}} = {\,^8}{C_r}{(2x)^{8 - r}}.{\left( {\frac{1}{x}} \right)^r} = {2^{8 - r}}{.^8}{C_r}.{x^{8 - 2r}}\]

Question: If the term independent of x in the expansion of \[{\left( {\sqrt x \, - \,\frac{\lambda }{{{x^2}}}} \right)^{10}}\] is 405, then λ equals
Solution:
\[{t_{r + 1}} = {\,^{10}}{C_r}\,{\left( {\sqrt x } \right)^{10 - r}}.\,{\left( { - \frac{\lambda }{{{x^2}}}} \right)^r} = {\left( { - \lambda } \right)^r}\,.{\,^{10}}{C_r}.\,{x^{5 - \frac{{5r}}{2}}}\]
for term independent of x, r = 2
\[\therefore {t_3} = {\left( { - \lambda } \right)^2}\,.{\,^{10}}{C_2} = 405\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,{\lambda ^2} = \frac{{405}}{{45}} = 9\]
\[\therefore \,\lambda = \, \pm \,3\]

Solution:

Question: If n,p ∈ N and in the expansion of (1 + x)<sup>n</sup> the coefficient of p<sup>th</sup> and (p + 1)<sup>th</sup> terms are respectively p and q. The p + q =
Solution:
Binomial coefficient of pth term is nCp–1 = p
Binomial coefficient of (p + 1)th term is nCp = q
\[ \Rightarrow \,\frac{{^n{C_{p - 1}}}}{{^n{C_p}}} = \frac{p}{q}\]
\[ \Rightarrow \,\frac{{n!}}{{(n - p + 1)\,!\,(p - 1)\,!}}\,.\,\frac{{(n - p)\,!\,p!}}{{n!}} = \frac{p}{q}\]
\[ \Rightarrow \frac{p}{{n - p + 1}} = \frac{p}{q}\]
⇒ n – p + 1 = q
⇒ p + q = n + 1

Sum of the coefficients :

no real values

a = 3

Solution:

Proceeding in same way given expresson = n+1Cr+1

Question: The coefficient of x<sup>50</sup> in the binomial expansion of (1 + x)<sup>1000</sup> + x(1 + x)<sup>999 </sup>+ x<sup>2</sup>(1 + x)<sup>998</sup> + ..... + x<sup>1000</sup> is:
Solution:
Given series is a G.P. with
\[a = {\left( {1 + x} \right)^{1000}},r = \frac{x}{{1 + x}}\]and n = 1001.
Formula for sum of GP upto n terms:
\[\frac{{a\,(1 - {r^n})}}{{(1 - r)}}\]
\[ = \frac{{{{(1 + x)}^{1000}}\left( {1 - {{\left( {\frac{x}{{x + 1}}} \right)}^{1001}}} \right)}}{{1 - \frac{x}{{x + 1}}}}\]
\[ = {\left( {1 + x} \right)^{1001}} - {x^{1001}}\]
Now, coefficient of x50 is 1001C50 = \[\frac{{1001\,!}}{{951!\,\,50!}}\]

Question:
Statement I : \[\sum\limits_{r = 0}^n {(r + 1)\;.{\;^n}{C_r}} = (n + 2)\;{2^{n - 1}}\]
Statement II : \[\sum\limits_{r = 0}^n {(r + 1){\;^n}{C_r}.{x^r} = {{(1 + x)}^n} + nx\;{{(1 + x)}^{n - 1}}} \]
Options: Statement I is false, Statement II is true
Statement I is true, Statement II is true;Statement II is a correct explanation for Statement I
Statement I is true, Statement II is true;Statement II is not a correct explanation for Statement I
Statement I is true, Statement II is false
Solution:
Using binomial expansion:
\[{\left( {1 + x} \right)^n} = {\,^n}{C_0} + {\,^n}{C_1}x + {\,^n}{C_2}{x^2} + ...... + {\,^n}{C_n}{x^n}\]
Multiply by ‘x’,
\[x{\left( {1 + x} \right)^n} = {\,^n}{C_0}x + {\,^n}{C_1}{x^2} + {\,^n}{C_2}{x^3} + ...... + {\,^n}{C_n}{x^{n + 1}}\]
Differentiate with respect to ‘x’,
\[ \Rightarrow x\,n{\left( {1 + x} \right)^{n - 1}} + {\left( {1 + x} \right)^n} = {\,^n}{C_0} + \,2{\,^n}{C_1}x + 3{\,^n}{C_2}{x^2} + ...... + \left( {n + 1} \right){\,^n}{C_n}{x^n}\]
Above is statement II.
Put x = 1
\[ \Rightarrow n{2^{n - 1}} + {2^n} = {\,^n}{C_0} + \,{2^n}{C_1} + .... + {\left( {n + 1} \right)^{\,\,n}}{C_n}\]
⇒ S II is correct explanation of SI.

Question: If \[\frac{1}{{8!}}\, + \frac{1}{{9!}} = \frac{x}{{10!}}\], then x is equal to
The coefficient of x<sup>3</sup> in \[{\left( {\sqrt {{x^5}} \, + \,\frac{3}{{\sqrt {{x^3}} }}} \right)^6}\] is :
Solution:
General term of the expansion is,
\[^6{C_r}\;{\left( {{x^{\frac{5}{2}}}} \right)^{6 - r}}{\left( {\frac{3}{{{x^{\frac{3}{2}}}}}} \right)^r}\]
\[^6{C_r}\;{x^{\left( {6 - r} \right)\frac{5}{2}}}\;.\;{3^r}\;.\;{x^{ - \frac{{3r}}{2}}}\]
Coefficient of x3 will be
⇒ 15 – 4r = 3
⇒ 12 = 4r
⇒ r = 3
Thus coefficient of x3 will be
6Cr . (3)r where r = 3
⇒ 6C3 . (3)3
⇒ 540

Question: The number of integral terms in the expansion of \[{\left( {\sqrt 3 \,\, + \,\sqrt 2 } \right)^{500}}\] is :
Solution:
General term :
\[ \Rightarrow \,{\,^{500}}{C_r}\,{(3)^{\frac{{500 - r}}{2}}}{(2)^{\frac{r}{2}}}\]
For integral terms r must be divisible by 2

⇒ 500 = 0 + (n – 1) × 2
⇒ 250 = n –1
⇒ n = 251

Question: a, b, c, d are any four consecutive co-efficients of any binomial expansion, then \[\frac{{a\, + \,b}}{a},\,\,\frac{{b\, + \,c}}{b},\,\,\frac{{c\, + \,d}}{c}\] are in:
Solution:
Let, a = nCr–2
b = nCr–1
c = nCr
d = nCr+1
\[ \Rightarrow \frac{a}{{a + b}} = \frac{{{\,^n}{C_{r - 2}}}}{{^n{C_{r - 2}} + {\,^n}{C_{r - 1}}}} = \frac{{^n{C_{r - 2}}}}{{^{n + 1}{C_{r - 2}}}} = \frac{{^n{C_{r - 2}}}}{{\frac{{n + 1}}{{r - 1}}{\,^n}{C_{r - 2}}}}\]
\[ \Rightarrow \frac{a}{{a + b}} = \frac{{r - 1}}{{n + 1}}\]
Similarly \[\frac{b}{{b + c}} = \,\frac{{{\,^n}{C_{r - 1}}}}{{^n{C_{r - 1}} + {\,^n}{C_r}}} = \frac{{^n{C_{r - 1}}}}{{^{n + 1}{C_r}}}\]
\[ \Rightarrow \frac{b}{{b + c}} = \,\frac{r}{{n + 1}}\]
\[and\frac{c}{{c + d}} = \,\frac{{^n{C_r}}}{{^n{C_r} + {\,^n}{C_{r + 1}}}} = \frac{{^n{C_r}}}{{^{n + 1}{C_{r + 1}}}}\]
\[ \Rightarrow \frac{c}{{c + d}} = \,\frac{{r + 1}}{{n + 1}}\]
Now, these terms are in A.P.
Hence \[\frac{{a + b}}{a},\frac{{b + c}}{b},\frac{{c + d}}{c}\]are in H.P.

Question: The relation of the isoelectric point for an amino acid, to solubility is
Options: an amino acid is least soluble at its isoelectric point
an amino acid has the maximum solubility at the isoelectric point
solubilities of only some amino acids depend on it.
Solution: The solubility of amino acids depend on the pH of the solution. Amino acid can be either positively or negatively charged overall due to the terminal amine -NH2 and carboxyl (-COOH) groups and the groups on the side chain. It is positively charged at low pH and negatively charged at high pH. The intermediate pH at which a protein molecule has a net charge of zero is called the isoelectric point of that protein. In general, the net charge on the protein, either positive or negative, can interact with water molecules, meaning that it is more likely water soluble. As a result, amino acid is the least soluble when the pH of the solution is at its isoelectric point.

Question:
A sample of carbohydrate X is tested for its functional groups:
1. It gives a positive Fehling's test and Tollen's test
2. It does not undergo hydrolysis.
3. Its molecular formula is C6H12O6.
If the compound has one asymmetric carbon atom, calculate the number of possible stereoisomers.
Solution:
1. Fehling's Test and Tollen's Test:
The positive results indicate X is a reducing sugar with an aldehyde group (–CHO).
2. No Hydrolysis:
This confirms that X is a monosaccharide, not a disaccharide or polysaccharide.
3. Molecular Formula C6H12O6:
This matches the general formula of an aldose monosaccharide (like glucose or its isomers).
4. One Asymmetric Carbon Atom:
The number of stereoisomers for a molecule is given by 2n, where n is the number of asymmetric (chiral) carbons.
Here, n = 1. So, the number of stereoisomers is:
21 = 2

Question: In glycine, the basic group at isoelectric point

An octapeptide (Mol. wt. = 516 g) on complete hydrolysis gives an alanine (Mol. mass =89 g). Alanine contributes 41.59% to total weight of hydrolysed product. How many numbers of alanine units are present in octapeptide?
Solution:
Molecular weight of octapeptide = 516 g/mole 
Total bonds to be hydrolysed = 8 – 1 =7 
Total weight of H2O added = 7 × 18 = 126 g/mol 
Total weight of hydrolysed product = 126 + 516 = 642 g 
Total weight of alanine in product = 624 × 41.59/100 = 267 g  
Molecular weight of alanine = 89 g/mole 
Number of alanine unit = 267/89 = 3

Question: Capacitor $A$ has a capacitance of $15\mu F$ when it is filled with a medium of dielectric constant 15. Another capacitor $B$ has a capacitance of $1\mu F$with air between the plates. Both are charged separately by a battery of$100\;V$. After charging, both are connected in parallel without the battery and the dielectric medium being removed. The common potential now is 100 m volts where m is:
Solution:
Charge on capacitor A is given by ${Q_1} = 15 \times {10^{ - 6}} \times 100 = 15 \times {10^{ - 4}}C$
Charge on capacitor B is given by ${Q_2} = 1 \times {10^{ - 6}} \times 100 = {10^{ - 4}}C$
Capacity of capacitor A after removing dielectric $ = \frac{{15 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}}}{{15}} = 1\mu F$
Now when both capacitors are connected in parallel their equivalent capacitance will be Ceq $ = 1 + 1 = 2\mu F$
So common potential $ = \frac{{(15 \times {{10}^{ - 4}}) + (1 \times {{10}^{ - 4}})}}{{2 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}}} = 800V.$

$\left( {4 + 2} \right)V = \left( {4 \times 50} \right) + \left( {2 \times 100} \right)$
$V = \frac{{400}}{6} = \frac{{200}}{3}V$
${U_{initial}} = \left( {\frac{1}{2} \times 4 \times {{\left( {50} \right)}^2} + \frac{1}{2} \times 2 \times {{\left( {1000} \right)}^2}} \right) \times {10^{ - 6}}$
$ = \left( {5000 + 10000} \right) \times {10^{ - 6}} = 1.5 \times {10^{ - 2}}J$
${U_{final}} = \frac{1}{2}\left[ {\left( {4 + 2} \right) \times {{10}^{ - 6}}} \right] \times \frac{{200}}{3} \times \frac{{200}}{3} = 1.33 \times {10^{ - 2}}J$

Question: A capacitor of 2μ F is charged as shown in the diagram. When the switch S is turned to position 2, the percentage of its stored energy dissipated is
Solution:
Initial energy stored in $2\mu F$ capacitor is
${U_i} = \frac{1}{2} \times 2{\left( V \right)^2} = {V^2}$
Final voltage after switch 2 is ON is
${V_f} = \frac{{{C_1}{V_1}}}{{{C_1} + {C_2}}} = \frac{{2V}}{{10}} = 0.2V$
Final energy in both the capacitors is
${U_f} = \frac{1}{2}\left( {{C_1} + {C_2}} \right)V_f^2 = \frac{1}{2}10{\left( {\frac{{2V}}{{10}}} \right)^2} = 0.2{V^2}$
So, energy dissipated $ = \frac{{{V^2} - 0.2{V^2}}}{{{V^2}}} \times 100 = 80\% $

Question: The plates of parallel plate capacitor are charged upto$100\;V$. A $2mm$ thick plate is inserted between the plates. Then to maintain the same potential difference, the distance between the plates is increased by$1.6\;mm$. The dielectric constant of the plate is
Solution:
In air the potential difference between the plates
${V_{air}} = \frac{\sigma }{{{\varepsilon _0}}}.d$ ..... (i)
In the presence of partially filled medium potential difference between the plates
${V_m} = \frac{\sigma }{{{\varepsilon _0}}}(d - t + \frac{t}{K})$ ..... (ii)
Potential difference between the plates with dielectric medium and increased distance is
${V_m}' = \frac{\sigma }{{{\varepsilon _0}}}\left\{ {(d + d') - t + \frac{t}{K}} \right\}$ ..... (iii)
According to question ${V_{air}} = {V_m}'$ which gives $K = \frac{t}{{t - d'}}$
Hence $K = \frac{2}{{2 - 1.6}} = 5$

wheat stone bridge.
\[{C_{eq}} = \frac{{{\varepsilon _0}A}}{d} = \frac{{8.85 \times {{10}^{ - 12}} \times 0.05}}{{17.7 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}\,F = 25\,pF.\]

Question:
The capacitance (C) for an isolated conducting sphere of radius (a) is given by $4\pi {\varepsilon _0}a$. This sphere is enclosed within an earthed concentric sphere. The ratio of the radii of the spheres being $\frac{n}{{\left( {n - 1} \right)}}$ then the capacitance of such a
sphere will be increased by a factor-
Solution:
$C = 4\pi { \in _0}a$
$C' = \frac{{4\pi { \in _0}ab}}{{b - a}} = \frac{{4\pi { \in _0}a}}{{1 - \frac{a}{b}}} = \frac{{4\pi { \in _0}a}}{{1 - \left( {\frac{{n - 1}}{n}} \right)}} = n\left( {4\pi { \in _0}a} \right) = nC$

Question: Shown is a network of infinite capacitors. Find the equivalent capacitance (in $\mu $F) across the end points P and Q. If your ans is $\lambda $, then write the value of 2$\lambda $ -7.5 (C = 3$\mu $F)
Solution:
If you look at the circuit between P and S then, its a balanced wheat stone bridge
${C_{PS}} = \frac{{3C \times 24C}}{{27C}} = \frac{{8C}}{3}$
Now capacitance in every subsequent wheat stone bridge is increased 32 times. Hence
$\frac{1}{{{C_{eq}}}} = \frac{3}{{8C}} + \frac{3}{{8C \times 32}} + \frac{3}{{8C \times {{32}^2}}}.......$
$ = \frac{3}{{8C}}\left[ {\frac{1}{{1 - \frac{1}{{32}}}}} \right] = \frac{3}{{8C}} \times \frac{{32}}{{31}} = \frac{{12}}{{31C}}$
${C_{eq}} = \frac{{31C}}{{12}}$

Question:

Extra charge that flow from battery after inserting dielectric slab $ = Q{'_B} - {Q_B} = 600\mu C - 120\mu C = 480\mu C$

Question: If the total heat dissipated in the circuit after a zero resistance wire is connected across the points A and B, is \[\frac{{2C{V^2}}}{n},\] the value of n is (Assume that capacitors were fully charged before the wires were connected)
Solution:
\[{V_1} = \frac{{\left( {2C} \right)V}}{{3C}} = \frac{2}{3}V\]
\[{U_1} = \frac{1}{2}C\frac{4}{9}{V^2} = \frac{{2C{V^2}}}{9}\]
\[\begin{gathered}
  {V_2} = \frac{V}{3} \\
  {U_2} = \frac{1}{2}\left( {2C} \right)\frac{{{V^2}}}{9} = \frac{{C{V^2}}}{9} \\
\end{gathered} \]
Initial total energy \[ = \frac{{C{V^2}}}{3}\]
Final total energy \[ = \frac{1}{2}\left( {2C} \right){V^2} = C{V^2}\]
Charge flow through the battery \[ = 2CV - \frac{2}{3}CV = \frac{4}{3}CV\]
\[ \Rightarrow {W_{battery}} = \frac{4}{3}C{V^2}\]
⇒ Heat dissipated \[ = \frac{{4C{V^2}}}{3} - \frac{{2C{V^2}}}{3} = \frac{2}{3}C{V^2}\]

$C = {C_1} + {C_2} = \frac{{13}}{{10}}\,\frac{{{\varepsilon _0}A}}{d} = \frac{{13{\varepsilon _0}\left( {0.1} \right)}}{{10\left( {\frac{{10}}{{1000}}} \right)}} = 13{\varepsilon _0}$

Question: A capacitor \[C = 10\mu F\] is connected across a battery of emf 10V. After it is charged to 10V, the battery is disconnected and reconnected across the capacitor with reversed polarity. The heat (in m J) dissipated in the circuit on reconnection is
Solution:
\[{W_{{\text{Battery}}}}\] = change in energy stored in capacitor $$ + $$ heat dissipated
Since Change in energy stored in capacitor is zero as C and V both don't change in value.
\[\therefore \] heat dissipated = \[{W_{{\text{Battery}}}}\]
\[{W_{{\text{Battery}}}}\] $$ = 2qV$$ because Initially if charge $$ + q$$ charge was on the plate then due to reverse polarity of battery, $$ - q$$ charge will appear on that plate which means total $$2q$$ charge was given by the battery. 

Now using $$q = CV$$ we get 

$${W_{{\text{Battery}}}} = 2C{V^2} = $$ Heat dissipated

Question:
A parallel plate capacitor of capacitance C is connected to a battery and is charged to a potential difference V. Another capacitor of capacitance 2C is connected to another battery
and is charged to potential difference 2V. The charging batteries are now disconnected and the capacitors are connected in parallel to each other in such a way that the positive terminal of one is connected to the negative terminal of the other. The final energy of the configuration is

${C_1}{V_1} = {C_2}{V_2} = \left( {{C_1} + {C_2}} \right){V_0}$
$2C\left( {2V} \right) - CV = \left( {C + 2C} \right){V_0}$
$3CV = 3C{V_0}$

$ \Rightarrow {V_0} = V$(Final pot. diff).
$\therefore \,\,{U_{final}} = \frac{1}{2}\left( {C + 2C} \right){V^2} = \frac{{3C{V^2}}}{2}$

Solution:
$C = \frac{{{ \in _0}ax}}{d} + \frac{{K{ \in _0}\left( {a - x} \right)a}}{d}$
$C = \frac{{K{ \in _0}{a^2}}}{d} - \frac{{{ \in _0}\left( {K - 1} \right)}}{d}x$ where $x = vt$
∴ $C - t$ graph is linear with negative slope.

Question: A parallel plate capacitor is made of two circular plates separated by a distance of 5 mm and with a dielectric of dielectric constant 2.2 between them. When the electric field in the dielectric is 3 × 104 V/m, the charge density of the positive plate will be close to :
Solution:
$E = \frac{\sigma }{{{\varepsilon _m}}} = \frac{\sigma }{{k{\varepsilon _o}}}$
$\sigma = k{\varepsilon _0}E = 2.2 \times 8.854 \times {10^{ - 12}} \times 3 \times {10^4}$
$\sigma = 5.87 \times {10^{ - 7}}\,C/{m^2}$

Question: Three plates A, B and C each of area 0.1 m2 are separated by 0.885 mm from each other as shown in the figure. A 10V battery is used to charge the system. The energy stored in the system is:
Solution:
Here the three plates will make (3 – 1) = 2 capacitors of same capacitance C and these two capacitors are connected in parallel.
The equivalent capacitance of the system is
${C_{eq}} = C + C = 2C$
Energy stored in this system is
$C = \frac{{A{\varepsilon _0}}}{d} = \frac{{0.1 \times 8.854 \times {{10}^{ - 12}}}}{{0.885 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}} = \ln F$
Hence ${C_{eq}} = 2nF$
$U = \frac{1}{2}{C_{eq}}{V^2} = \frac{1}{2} \times 2 \times {10^{ - 9}} \times {10^2} = {10^{ - 7}} = {10^{ - 1}}\mu J$

Question: A parallel plate capacitor of capacitance 200μF is charged by a battery of 25 V. The battery remains connected and if the plates of the capacitor are moved so that the distance between them is reduced to half of the original distance, the change in the energy of the capacitor is 'X' milli-joules. Find the value of 10X.
Solution:
Initial charge on capacitor \[Q = CV\]
Final charge =\[Q' = C'V = 2CV\]
Charge flows from the battery \[Q = CV\]
Energy given to capacitor $ = \frac{1}{2}{C_f}{V^2} - \frac{1}{2}{C_i}{V^2}$
$ = \frac{1}{2} \times C{V^2}$
$ = \frac{1}{2} \times 200 \times {10^{ - 6}} \times {25^2}$
$ = 625 \times {10^{ - 4}}{\text{J}}$

$ = 62.5 \times {10^{ - 3}}{\text{J}}$

X= 62.5 and 10X= 625

Question:
A capacitor of capacitance 1 mF withstands the maximum voltage 6 kV while a capacitor of 2mF withstands the maximum voltage 4 kV. What maximum voltage will the
system of these two capacitor withstands if they are connected in series ?
Solution:
Breaking voltage 6kV 4kV
Max (Breaking) charge 6μ C 8μ C
Max Safe charge 6μ C 6μ C
(Minimum Value)
Hence safe voltage $\frac{{6\mu C}}{{1\mu F}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\frac{{6\mu C}}{{2\mu F}}$
So total voltage = 6 + 3 = 9V

Question: A parallel plate capacitor with a slab of dilectric constant 3 filling the whole space between the plates is charged to certain potential and isolated. Then the slab is drawn out and another slab of equal thickness but dielectric constant 2 is introduced between the plates. The ratio of the energy stored in the capacitor later to that stored initially is
Solution:
$U = \frac{1}{2}\,\frac{{{Q^2}}}{C}$
When capacitor is charged using battery and Isolated, charge stored will remains same.
$\frac{{{U_1}}}{{{U_2}}} = \frac{{{C_2}}}{{{C_1}}} = \frac{{{k_2}}}{{{k_2}}} = \frac{2}{3}$

So the value of $\frac{{{U_2}}}{{{U_1}}} = \frac{3}{2}$

Question: Half portion of a spherical capacitor is filled with a dielectric of dielectric constant k = 2 and conductivity σ. The charge given to spherical capacitor is Q0. Due to the conductivity of dielectric charge leaks. The time constant for the discharge circuit is 'x' time of $\frac{{{\varepsilon _0}}}{\sigma }.$ Find the value of x.
Solution:
Let Co be the capacitance of the two shells,

$$C = \frac{{{\varepsilon _0}A}}{{2d}} + \frac{{k{\varepsilon _0}A}}{{2d}} = \frac{{3{C_o}}}{2}$$

$$dR = \frac{{dx}}{{2\pi \sigma {x^2}}}$$

Integrate

$$R = \frac{{b - a}}{{2\pi \sigma ab}}$$

$$C = \frac{3}{2} \times \frac{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}ab}}{{b - a}}$$

$$\bar C = RC = \frac{{b - a}}{{2\pi \sigma ab}} \times \frac{3}{2}\frac{{4\pi {\varepsilon _0}ab}}{{b - a}}$$

$$\bar C = RC = \frac{{3{\varepsilon _0}}}{\sigma }$$

$$\therefore \boxed{x = 3}$$

Question:
Two capacitors marked 10μ F, 400 volt and 4μ F, 100 volt are connected in series. What is the maximum safe potential that can be applied when these capacitor are joined in
series.
Solution:
${Q_1} = 4000\,\mu C\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{Q_2} = 400\,\mu C$
Maximum charge can be provided to both capacitor is $400\,\mu C$
${C_{eq}} = \frac{{40}}{{14}}\,\mu F;\,\,\,Q = {C_{eq}}.V$
$V = \frac{{400 \times 14}}{{40}} = 140V.$
$400 = \frac{{40}}{{14}}V$
$ \Rightarrow V = 140V$

Question:
The capacities of two conductors are C1 and C2 and their respective potentials are V1
 and V2. If they are connected by a thin wire then the loss of energy will be
Solution:
Total energy before sharing charges
${U_1} = \frac{1}{2}{C_1}V_1^2 + \frac{1}{2}{C_2}V_2^2$
Total energy after sharing charges
${U_2} = \frac{1}{2}\left( {{C_1} + {C_2}} \right){V^2}$
Common Potential Reached
$V = \frac{{{C_1}{V_1} + {C_2}{V_2}}}{{{C_1} + {V_2}}}$
${U_2} = \frac{1}{2}\left( {{C_1} + {C_2}} \right){\left[ {\frac{{{C_1}{V_1} + {C_2}{V_2}}}{{{C_1} + {C_2}}}} \right]^2} = \frac{{{{\left( {{C_1}{V_1} + {C_2}{V_2}} \right)}^2}}}{{2\left( {{C_1} + {C_2}} \right)}}$
Loss in energy $ = {U_1} - {U_2} = \frac{{{C_1}{C_2}{{\left( {{V_1} - {V_2}} \right)}^2}}}{{2\left( {{C_1} + {C_2}} \right)}}$

Question: The capacity of a parallel plate capacitor is C. Its capacitance when the separation between the plate is halved will be
Solution:
$C = \frac{{{ \in _o}A}}{d}$
$d' = \frac{d}{2} \Rightarrow C' = \frac{{{ \in _o}A}}{{d'}}$
$C' = 2\frac{{{ \in _o}A}}{d}$
$C' = 2C$

Question: The area of each plate of a parallel plate capacitor is $100\,c{m^2}$and the distance between the plates is$1mm$. It is filled with mica of dielectric constant 6. The radius (in m) of the sphere with equivalent capacitance will be (close to the nearest integer) :

r = Radius of sphere of equivalent capacitance

Question: In the figure shown the plates of a parallel plate capacitor have unequal charges. Its capacitance is 'C'. P is a point outside the capacitor and close to the plate of charge Q. The distance between the plates is 'd' then which statement is wrong
Options: A point charge at point 'P' will experience electric force due to capacitor
The potential difference between the plates will be $\frac{{3Q}}{{2C}}$
The energy stored in the electric field in the region between the plates is $\frac{{9{Q^2}}}{{8C}}$
Solution:
Force on point 'P' due to capacitor ≠ 0
(As the outer surface charges will create electric field at P.)
Potential diff. between the plates $ = \frac{{3Q}}{{2C}}$
Energy stored in electric field between the plates
$ = \frac{1}{2}C \times {\left( {\frac{{3Q}}{{2C}}} \right)^2} = \frac{{9{Q^2}}}{{8C}}$

Question: A parallel plate capacitor is made of two plates of length l, width $\omega $ and separated by distance d. A dielectric slab (dielectric constant K) that fits exactly between the plates is held near the edge of the plates. It is pulled into the capacitor by a force $F = - \frac{{\delta u}}{{\delta x}}$ where U is the energy of the capacitor when dielectric is inside the capacitor up to distance x (See figure). If the charge on the capacitor is Q then the force on the dielectric when it is near the edge is:
Solution:
As ${C_1}$ and ${C_2}$ are parallel
${C_1} = \frac{{k{\varepsilon _0}xw}}{d}$
${C_2} = \frac{{{\varepsilon _o}\left( {1 - x} \right)w}}{d}$
${C_{eq}} = {C_1} + {C_2} = \frac{{{\varepsilon _0}w}}{d}\left[ {l + x\left( {k - 1} \right)} \right]$
$U = \frac{{{Q^2}}}{{2C}} = \frac{{{Q^2}}}{{\frac{{2{\varepsilon _0}w}}{d}\left[ {l + x\left( {k - 1} \right)} \right]}}$
Force on dielectric is given by
$F = - \frac{{\partial U}}{{\partial x}}$
$F = \frac{{{Q^2}d}}{{2{\varepsilon _0}w}}\left[ {\frac{1}{{\left[ {l + x\left( {k - 1} \right)} \right]}} \times \left( {k - 1} \right)} \right]$
At x = 0
${F_{x = 0}} = \frac{{{Q^2}d\left( {k - 1} \right)}}{{2{\varepsilon _0}w{l^2}}}$

Question:
A parallel plate capacitor with air between the plates has a capacitance of 9 pF. The separation between its plates is d. The space between the plate is now filled with two dielectrics. One of the dielectrics has dielectric constant k1= 3 and thickness d/3 while the other one has dielectric constant k2 = 6 and thickness 2d/3. Capacitance of the
capacitor is now
Solution:
$C = \frac{{{\varepsilon _0}A.3}}{d} = 9 \times {10^{ - 12}}\,F$
With dielectric
${C_1} = \frac{{{\varepsilon _0}A.3}}{{\frac{d}{3}}} = 9C;\,\,\,{C_2} = \frac{{{\varepsilon _0}A.6}}{{\frac{{2d}}{3}}} = 9C$
$\therefore \,\,{C_{total}} = \frac{{{C_1}{C_2}}}{{{C_1} + {C_2}}}$ as they are in series.
$ = \frac{{9C \times 9C}}{{18C}} = \frac{9}{2}\, \times C$ or $\frac{9}{2} \times 9 \times {10^{ - 12}}F$
$ \Rightarrow {C_{total}} = 40.5pF$

Question:
A 40 µF capacitor in a defibrillator is charged to 3000 V. The energy stored in the capacitor is sent through the patient during a pulse of duration 2 ms. The power delivered to
the patient is :
Solution: $P = \frac{{\Delta U}}{{\Delta t}} = \frac{{\frac{1}{2}C{V^2}}}{{\Delta t}} = \frac{{\frac{1}{2} \times 40 \times {{10}^{ - 6}} \times 9 \times {{10}^6}}}{{2 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}} = 90kW$

Question: The charge on a capacitor is 30 μC. It is now connected to another capacitor of half of its capacity in parallel then the charge on the first capacitor (in μC) is

$\frac{{30 - {\text{Q}}}}{{\text{C}}} = \frac{{2{\text{Q}}}}{{\text{C}}}$
⇒  3Q = 30
      Q = 10
So, charge on the first capacitor will be q = 30-10 = 20

Question: Three condensers of capacities 3 mF, 6 mF, 12mF are connected in series with a battery. If the charge on 12 mF condenser is 24 mC, the P.D. across the battery is(in Voltts)

Q must be same on all capacitors, because they all in series
And \[\frac{1}{{{C_{net}}}} = \frac{1}{3} + \frac{1}{6} + \frac{1}{{12}} = \frac{7}{{12}}\]
Cnet = 12/7,
and Q = 24
Q = Cnet V
V = 24(7/12) = 14

(D)

is

Electron withdrawing groups increase the acid strength and electron donating groups decrease the acid strength. So, we get the correct order of increasing acid strength as CF3CO2H > MeOCH2CO2H > CH3CO2H > (Me)2CHCO2H.
So , the correct option is (c).

Question:
The correct order of increasing acid strength of the compounds
A) CH3CO2H
B) MeOCH2CO2H
C) CF3CO2H
D)

is
Solution:
Electron withdrawing groups increase the acid strength and electron donating groups decrease the acid strength. So, we get the correct order of increasing acid strength as CF3CO2H > MeOCH2CO2H > CH3CO2H > (Me)2CHCO2H.
So, the correct option is (c).

Question:
Consider the acidity of the carboxylic acids :
(i) C6H5COOH
(ii) o–NO2C6H4COOH
(iii) p–NO2C6H4COOH
(iv) m–NO2C6H4COOH
Which of the following order is correct ?
Solution:
Electron withdrawing group increases the acidity of benzoic acid, o-isomer will have higher acidity then corresponding m and p isomer due to ortho-effect. As M group (i.e. NO2) at p- position have more pronounced electron withdrawing effect than as
-NO2 group at m- position (-I effect)
∴ Correct order of acidity is ii > iii > iv > i.

Question: A bullet of mass 0.01 kg and travelling at a speed of \[500\,m{s^{ - 1}}\] strikes a block of 2 kg, which is suspended by a string of length 5 m. The centre of gravity of the block is found to rise through a vertical height of 0.1 m. The speed of the bullet after it emerges from the block is
Solution:
For block from initial to final position.

We can apply conservation of Mechanical energy.

\[M{E_i} = M{E_f}\]

\[\frac{1}{2}mv_1^2 = mgh\]

\[v_1^2 = 2gh\]

\[H = 0.1m\]

\[{V_1} = \sqrt {2(9.8)\;(0.1)} \]

\[{V_1} = \sqrt 2 = 1.4\]

Now for collision

\[{\vec P_i} = {\vec P_f}\]

0.01 (500) = 2v1 + 0.01v2

5 = 2 (1.4) + 0.01

\[{V_2} \Rightarrow \,{V_2} = 220m/s\]

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Chapter: Centre of mass, Momentum and Collision

Question: Two blocks of masses 10 kg and 4 kg are connected by a spring of negligible mass and placed on a frictionless horizontal surface.  An impulse gives a velocity of 14 m/s to the heavier block in the direction of the lighter block.  The velocity of the centre of mass (in m/s) is
Solution: Just after collision \[{V_c}\, = \frac{{10x14 + 4x0}}{{10 + 4}} = 10\]m/s; since spring force is internal force, it cannot change the linear momentum of the (two mass + spring)system.  V<sub>c</sub> remains the same.

Question: Three rods of the same mass are placed as shown in the figure. What will be the co-ordinate of centre of mass of the system ?
Solution:
CH of vertical rod will be \[\left( {0,\;\frac{a}{2}} \right)\]

CH of horizontal rod will be \[\left( {\frac{a}{2},0} \right)\]

CH of inclined rod will be \[\left( {\frac{0}{a},\;\frac{a}{2}} \right)\]

So, the centre of mass of the system will be

at\[x = \frac{{m\left( {0 + 0.5a + 0.5a} \right)}}{{m + m + m}} = \frac{a}{3}\]

and y \[x = \frac{{m\left( {0.5a + 0 + 0.5a} \right)}}{{m + m + m}} = \frac{a}{3}\]

or at \[\left( {\frac{a}{3},\;\frac{a}{3}} \right)\]

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Chapter: Centre of mass, Momentum and Collision
Tags.Topic: Centre of mass of discrete system

Question: A particle of mass m moving towards the east with speed v collides with another particle of the same mass and same speed v moving towards the north. If the two particles stick to each other, the new particle of mass 2m will have a speed of
Solution:
After collision


Before collision

By conservation of linear momentum.

\[{\vec P_i} = {\vec P_r}\]

\[mv\hat i + mv\hat j = 2m{\vec V_0}\]

\[ \Rightarrow \;{\vec V_0} = \frac{v}{2}\hat i + \frac{v}{2}\hat j\]

\[\therefore \;\left| {{{\vec V}_0}} \right| = \sqrt {{{\left( {\frac{v}{2}} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {\frac{v}{2}} \right)}^2}} \]

\[\left| {{{\vec V}_0}} \right| = \frac{v}{{\sqrt 2 }}\]

Question: A shell of mass m moving with velocity v suddenly breaks into 2 pieces. The part having mass m/4 remains stationary. The velocity of other part will be
Solution:
\[{\vec F_{net}}\]on system = 0

\[\therefore \;\vec P\;{\text{system}} = {\text{conserved}}\]

\[\therefore \;{\vec P_i} = {\vec P_f}\]

\[mv = \frac{m}{4}(0) + \frac{{3m}}{4}v'\]

\[v' = \frac{4}{3}v\]

Question: A 4 kg mass and a 1 kg mass are moving with equal kinetic energies. The ratio of their momenta is
Solution:
\[{m_1} = 4,{m_2} = 1\]

\[P = \sqrt {2mKE} \]

\[KE = \sqrt {2mKE} \]

\[P\;\alpha \sqrt m \]

\[\frac{{{P_1}}}{{{P_2}}} = \sqrt {\frac{{{m_1}}}{{{m_2}}}} = \sqrt {\frac{4}{1}} = 2:1\]

Question: A body of mass $m = 0.10$ kg has an initial velocity of $3\hat i$ m/s. It collides elastically with another body, B of the mass which has an initial velocity of ${x_0}$. After collision, A moves with a velocity $v = 4\left( {\hat i + \hat j} \right)$ m/s. The energy of B after collision is written as $\frac{x}{{10}}J$, the value of $x$ is
Solution:
Mass of each object, ${m_1} = {m_2} = 0.1\,kg$

Initial velocity of 1st object, ${\mu _1} = 5\,m/s$

Initial velocity of 2nd object, ${\mu _2} = 5\,m/s$

Final velocity of 1st object,

${V_1} = 4\hat i + 4\hat j\,m/s = \sqrt {{4^2} + {4^2}} = 16\sqrt 2 \,\,m/s$

For elastic collision, kinetic energy remains conserved Initial kinetic energy $\left( {{K_i}} \right) = $ Final kinetic energy $\left( {{K_f}} \right)$

$\frac{1}{2}m{u_1}^2 + \frac{1}{2}m{u_2}^2 = \frac{1}{2}m{v_1}^2 + \frac{1}{2}m{v_2}^2$

$\frac{1}{2}m{\left( 5 \right)^2} + \frac{1}{2}m{\left( 3 \right)^2} + \frac{1}{2}m{\left( {16\sqrt 2 } \right)^2} + \frac{1}{2}m{v_2}^2$

${V_2} = \sqrt 2 \,\,m/s$

Kinetic energy of second object $ = \frac{1}{2}m{v_2}^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 0.1 \times {\sqrt 2 ^2} = \frac{1}{{10}}$

$ \Rightarrow x = 1$

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Chapter: Centre of mass, Momentum and Collision

Question: A bullet weighing 50 gm leaves the gun with a velocity of 30 m/s. If the recoil speed imparted to the gun is 1 m/s, the mass of the gun is :
Solution:
\[{\vec P_i} = {\vec P_f}\]

\[0 = 50(30) - M\left( {{V_g}} \right)\]

\[M\left( 1 \right) = 50\left( {30} \right)\]

\[M = 1500gm\]

\[M = 1.5kg\]

Question: A ball collides elastically with another ball of the same mass. The collision is oblique and initially one of the balls was at rest. After the collision, the two balls move with same speeds. What will be angle (in degrees) between the velocities of the balls after the collision?
Solution:
Applying law of conservation of momentum, we get

\[mv\,\sin {\theta _1} - mv\sin {\theta _2} = 0\]

\[ \Rightarrow {\theta _1} = {\theta _2}\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( i \right)\]

Also,

\[mu = mv\left( {\cos {\theta _1} + \cos {\theta _2}} \right)\]

\[ = 2\,mv\cos \theta \left[ {{\theta _1} = \theta \left( {say} \right)} \right]\]

\[\cos \theta = \frac{u}{{2v}}\,\,\,\left( {ii} \right)\]

According to law of conservation of KE,

\[\frac{1}{2}m{u^2} = \frac{1}{2}m{v^2} + \frac{1}{2}m{v^2}\]

\[{u^2} = 2{v^2}\,or\,u = \sqrt 2 \,v\,\,\,\left( {iii} \right)\]

\[{\text{or}}\,\,\cos \theta = \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }}\,\,\,{\text{or}}\,\,\,\theta = 45^\circ \]

\[\therefore \,{\theta _1} + {\theta _2} = 90^\circ \]

Tags.Subject: Physics
Tags.Chapter: Centre of mass, Momentum and Collision

Question: Three identical balls each of mass 5 kg are connected with each other as shown in figure, and rests over a smooth horizontal table. At moment t = 0 ball B is given velocity 9 m/sec then velocity of A in direction of velocity of B just before collision with C in m/s?
